title: EE-REHEARSE-001: Add isolated rehearsal sandbox for EE command sequences
description:
Define and implement an agent-native isolated rehearsal sandbox for EE command sequences.

Background:
The bead graph already has strong safety primitives: dry-run-first commands, command-effect manifests, audit timelines, support bundles, handoff capsules, backup/restore-to-side-path, no-mutation contract tests, and detailed e2e logging. Those pieces still leave one real-world gap for agents: before applying a risky or multi-step EE workflow, an agent cannot ask EE to rehearse the exact sequence against an isolated copy of EE state and report the resulting effect diff.

This bead adds that missing plan-space-to-action bridge. Agents should be able to validate a proposed EE workflow against a temporary side-path database/index/config snapshot, observe what would change, inspect degraded behavior, and decide whether to run the real commands. This improves user safety without removing any existing mutating command, dry-run contract, backup flow, or audit behavior.

Intended public surface:
- `ee rehearse plan --json --commands <file|json> [--workspace <id>] [--profile quick|full|privacy]` validates a command-sequence spec, resolves scope, estimates required artifacts, and reports whether rehearsal can run.
- `ee rehearse run --json --commands <file|json> [--out <dir>] [--workspace <id>] [--profile quick|full|privacy]` creates an isolated side-path copy, runs only declared EE commands inside that copy, and returns a manifest with expected vs observed effects.
- `ee rehearse inspect <artifact-or-run-id> --json` validates a prior rehearsal artifact, hashes, schemas, redaction posture, changed-surface summaries, and replay hints.
- `ee rehearse promote-plan <artifact-or-run-id> --json` emits a non-mutating ordered plan for how an agent could run the real commands, including preflight warnings, required confirmations, backup/audit requirements, and stop conditions.

This is not a shell runner, not an automatic apply engine, not an undo system, not a backup replacement, and not a way to bypass command-effect restrictions. It rehearses EE command specs in a disposable local side path and produces evidence.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including comprehensive unit tests, real-binary e2e scripts, schema/golden fixtures, privacy checks, command-effect checks, and detailed logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Model rehearsals as versioned command-sequence specs, not free-form shell strings. A spec should name EE command path, args, expected effect class, required workspace scope, idempotency key if relevant, allowed degraded states, and stop-on-failure behavior.
- Reuse existing state primitives: workspace resolution, backup/restore-to-side-path, transaction helper, command-effect manifest, audit repository, operation audit timeline, response envelope, redaction policy, fixture manifest, and failure replay artifacts. Do not create a parallel storage, audit, export, or restore mechanism.
- The real workspace must be opened only for read/snapshot purposes. All command execution in `run` happens against an explicit temporary or user-provided side-path EE home/workspace copy. Generated derived artifacts stay under the rehearsal output root or the side-path copy.
- Treat the rehearsal artifact as first-class evidence: manifest schema, run ID, source workspace ID, source state hash, side-path location, command list hash, expected/observed effects, changed tables/artifacts, redaction summary, degradation codes, operation IDs inside the sandbox, elapsed timings, logs, and next commands.
- `promote-plan` is read-only. It must never apply the real commands. It should produce a careful checklist with exact command specs, required preconditions, recommended backup/audit checks, stop conditions, and expected operation-audit queries after real execution.
- Keep arbitrary shell out of scope. Rehearsal may invoke only EE command handlers through the same internal service layer used by the CLI/MCP adapters, with a capability-narrowed context pointed at the sandbox state.
- Make non-rehearsable commands explicit. Commands requiring live external I/O, unsupported workspace-file mutation, unsafe raw export, or missing effect manifest entries should return stable refusal/degradation codes with next actions rather than being silently skipped.
- Keep output compact by default and artifact-backed for detail. Stdout is the canonical `ee.response.v1` payload; progress, command traces, copied paths, redaction events, and first-failure diagnosis go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- `ee rehearse plan --json` validates a command-sequence spec without mutating EE state, resolves workspace scope, checks command-effect manifest coverage, estimates side-path/output needs, reports non-rehearsable commands with stable codes, and emits next actions.
- `ee rehearse run --json` creates or uses an explicit isolated side-path copy, executes only declared EE command specs against that copy, and returns a manifest with run ID, source workspace ID, source state hash, command list hash, expected effects, observed effects, sandbox operation IDs, changed tables/artifacts, degradation codes, redaction posture, artifact paths, elapsed timing, and replay/inspect commands.
- `ee rehearse inspect <artifact-or-run-id> --json` validates schema version, manifest hashes, command spec hash, source/sandbox state hashes, referenced artifacts, redaction summaries, expected/observed effect records, and supported version ranges.
- `ee rehearse promote-plan <artifact-or-run-id> --json` is read-only and emits exact real-run command specs, required backups or audit prechecks, stop conditions, expected operation-audit queries, and warnings for differences between sandbox and real state.
- The real EE workspace/database/index/config is not mutated by plan, run, inspect, or promote-plan except for explicitly declared rehearsal artifacts under `--out` or an EE-controlled temporary artifact root.
- Rehearsal refuses or degrades honestly for commands with external I/O, unknown effect manifest entries, unsupported workspace-file writes, missing backup/restore capability, ambiguous workspace scope, corrupted source state, stale schema versions, and redaction failure.
- Rehearsal artifacts can be cited by support bundles, handoff capsules, closure dossiers, failure replay artifacts, audit timeline entries, and e2e logs through stable IDs and content hashes.
- Public schemas and golden fixtures cover valid plan, non-rehearsable command, successful run, failed command with preserved artifacts, partial degraded run, inspect success, inspect hash mismatch, promote-plan success, ambiguous workspace, unsupported future schema, and privacy profile redaction.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add unit tests for command-sequence spec parsing, command allow/refuse classification, workspace scope resolution, side-path selection, state-hash calculation, manifest hashing, effect comparison, redaction summary aggregation, non-rehearsable code mapping, promote-plan generation, and read-only mutation checks.
- Add integration tests against temporary FrankenSQLite/SQLModel databases covering empty workspace, initialized workspace, multiple workspaces with ambiguity, stale index, corrupted source state, duplicate idempotency keys, rollback/failure inside sandbox, and unsupported schema versions.
- Add real-binary e2e coverage that initializes an isolated workspace, records/imports sample memory, rehearses a multi-step remember/search/context/curate-like sequence in a side path, inspects the artifact, emits a promote-plan, and proves the source workspace state hash is unchanged.
- Add a risky-command e2e scenario where rehearsal refuses a command with missing effect manifest coverage or unsupported external I/O and returns stable next actions without mutating source state.
- Add privacy regression tests with synthetic secrets in command specs, memory bodies, session-like text, config-like values, paths, stdout/stderr artifacts, and sandbox manifests; raw synthetic secrets must not appear in stdout, artifacts intended for handoff/support, promote plans, or failure summaries.
- Add adversarial/property coverage for malformed command specs, path traversal in `--commands` and `--out`, invalid UTF-8/control characters, duplicate command IDs, huge command lists, stale run IDs, symlinked output paths, concurrent rehearsals, and sandbox cleanup failures.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, source workspace ID, sandbox path, output artifact path, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, manifest hash, command-list hash, source-before/source-after hashes, expected/observed effect summary, redaction status, schema/golden validation status, and first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; progress, copy decisions, skipped command explanations, redaction events, side-path diagnostics, cleanup warnings, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- Closeout evidence must include fixture IDs, scenario IDs, manifest hashes, command-effect manifest entries, exact verification commands, source-state unchanged proof, and a clear explanation of how rehearsal differs from dry-run, backup, support bundles, handoff capsules, and undo.
notes: Added during repeated bead quality review after searches found dry-run/effect/audit/backup support but no general sandbox/rehearsal command. This is intentionally accretive: it preserves every planned feature while giving agents a safe way to test EE command sequences against an isolated side-path copy before touching real state.
close_reason: Added rehearse e2e JSON/output coverage (commit f2b29d3)

title: Epic: Output Encoding And TOON
description:
Epic for the `Output Encoding And TOON` workstream.

        Background and intent:
        Add optional TOON rendering as a token-saving output format while keeping JSON canonical for tests, schemas, hooks, MCP, audit, and storage. TOON must never become hidden state.

        How this serves the project:
        This workstream is one layer of the EE memory substrate. It should be implemented only through its child beads, preserving the constraints and evidence-first design of the root program bead.

        Cross-cutting constraints:
        Global implementation invariants for this task:
- Keep the CLI local-first, deterministic, and agent-native; stdout is data, stderr is diagnostics.
- Preserve the no-Tokio and no-rusqlite constraint unless a later ADR explicitly changes the architecture.
- Use SQLModel Rust plus FrankenSQLite for durable data; do not introduce a second storage stack.
- Use Asupersync for async edges and cancellation/budget behavior; do not smuggle in a Tokio executor.
- Keep JSON as the canonical public contract. TOON, Markdown, diagrams, and MCP are renderers or adapters over the same domain data.
- Mutating commands need dry-run/idempotency/audit behavior unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- Add focused tests proportional to risk: unit tests for domain logic, contract/golden tests for public output, and integration/evaluation tests for cross-subsystem behavior.

## Success Criteria

Detailed epic closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as the epic's success contract, including cross-bead user validation, evidence, and logging requirements.
design: Coordinate the `Output Encoding And TOON` implementation tasks. Keep this epic blocked on its child tasks so epic status reflects real implementation progress. Do not implement code directly under this epic without creating or claiming a more specific child bead.
acceptance_criteria:
## Success Criteria

All child beads in `Output Encoding And TOON` are closed, relevant contract/golden/evaluation tests pass, and any category-specific readiness gate is green.

Epic-level user validation requirements:
- Before this epic closes, its child beads that expose user/agent behavior must have concrete unit, golden/schema, e2e, evaluation, replay, or scenario evidence appropriate to their risk.
- The close reason or final comment must summarize implemented user-visible workflows, verification commands, artifact/log locations, degraded states covered, and any intentionally postponed scope with follow-up bead IDs.
- Do not close this epic by code completion alone; close it only when cross-bead integration is demonstrated and no planned feature/functionality was silently dropped.
- If a child bead closes without executable tests, the epic owner must verify that the exception is documented and that nearest-neighbor scenario or contract coverage still protects the user outcome.
notes: This is a planning/coordination epic, not a direct coding task. It exists to keep the generated bead graph self-documenting.

design:
Suggested design approach:
- Tests are part of the deliverable, not a follow-up. Golden output should be deterministic with fixed IDs, timestamps, ordering, and schema versions.

Implementation guidance:
- Read the surrounding modules and existing project conventions before editing.
- Prefer the planned crate boundaries from the comprehensive plan: CLI wiring in `ee-cli`, reusable domain/services in `ee-core`, models in `ee-models`, persistence in `ee-db`, output rendering in `ee-output`, and adapters in their own optional crates.

- If a dependency or upstream Franken project is not ready, implement the smallest honest diagnostic/degraded path rather than faking behavior.
- Document any architectural exception with an ADR-oriented note and a verification hook.
acceptance_criteria:

title: Create causal credit review skill over ee causal ledgers
description:
## Background

Causal interpretation requires judgment about confounders, evidence tiers, and whether a measured association should affect memory priority. The CLI can expose ledgers and conservative stats; the skill should perform the review and produce recommendations for a human/agent to accept or reject.

## Acceptance Criteria

See this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field for the detailed completion contract, including unit/static checks, e2e or docs/e2e coverage, detailed logging, and no-feature-loss requirements.
design: The skill should consume `ee causal trace/estimate/compare/promote-plan --json` as mechanical evidence. It should require sample sizes, assumptions, confounders, baseline/candidate definitions, and safety guard checks before making recommendations.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- Skill lives under `skills/causal-credit-review/` or equivalent.
- Workflow gates require sufficient evidence tier before recommending promote/demote/reroute.
- Underpowered/confounded estimates route to review or learning-experiment suggestions, not direct action.
- Output separates mechanical measurements, interpretation, recommendation, risks, and required follow-up `ee` commands.
- Includes templates for causal review memo, confounder checklist, and evidence-tier rubric.
- Unit/static tests validate required `SKILL.md` sections, evidence-tier gates, confounder checklist fields, underpowered/confounded refusal behavior, recommendation wording constraints, and output section/schema checks.
- E2E skill fixture covers no evidence, confounded evidence, underpowered evidence, replay-supported evidence, experiment-supported evidence, redacted evidence, and degraded causal-ledger output with detailed logs.
- E2E logs include skill path, fixture ID/hash, causal ledger/evidence IDs, evidence bundle path/hash, evidence tier, redaction/degraded status, recommendation class, output artifact path, required-section check, and first-failure diagnosis.
close_reason: Created causal-credit-review skill with SKILL.md, references, fixtures, and validation script

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Mechanical-boundary reconciliation eidetic_engine_cli-o5l2: this skill owns causal interpretation over mechanical causal ledgers from dz00. It may recommend promote/demote/reroute only through explicit evidence-tier gates and must separate measurements, assumptions, risks, interpretation, and follow-up commands in e2e fixtures.

title: Gate 3: Asupersync Contract Fixtures
description:
Readiness gate from the comprehensive EE plan.

Gate text and intent:
Create deterministic LabRuntime fixtures:

```text
tests/contracts/asupersync_budget.rs
tests/contracts/asupersync_cancellation.rs
tests/contracts/asupersync_quiescence.rs
```

Pass criteria:

- budget exhaustion maps to the documented CLI outcome
- `Outcome::Cancelled` survives service layers and maps to a documented cancellation exit
- `Outcome::Panicked` is never treated as retryable domain failure
- a cancelled `remember`, `search`, or `context` path has no orphan tasks and no partial durable writes
- region close implies quiescence

Why this matters:
Gates prevent roadmap gravity from turning aspirational features into unverified claims. Closing this bead means the implementation has executable evidence, not just code that compiles.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.

- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.

close_reason: Completed Gate 3 Asupersync contract fixture coverage. Existing fixtures cover budget exhaustion CLI outcome mapping, cancellation propagation and exit mapping, panicked outcome classification, cancelled command no-orphan/no-write probes, deterministic LabRuntime quiescence, and region close quiescence. Adjusted the quiescence fixture to assert the stable public state/quiescence contract instead of the unavailable close_outcome API. Verification: rustfmt --edition 2024 --check tests/contracts/asupersync_quiescence.rs; git diff --check on contract files; UBS_SKIP_RUST_BUILD=1 ubs --skip-type-narrowing --only=rust on the four contract files; RCH remote cargo test --test contracts asupersync -- --nocapture passed on ovh-a, 9/9; RCH remote cargo clippy --test contracts -- -D warnings passed on ovh-a. - Keep JSON as the canonical public contract. TOON, Markdown, diagrams, and MCP are renderers or adapters over the same domain data.
- Mutating commands need dry-run/idempotency/audit behavior unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- Add focused tests proportional to risk: unit tests for domain logic, contract/golden tests for public output, and integration/evaluation tests for cross-subsystem behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Suggested design approach:
- Keep scope narrow but complete: implement the named behavior, wire it into the relevant command/schema/test surfaces, and avoid unrelated refactors.

Implementation guidance:
- Read the surrounding modules and existing project conventions before editing.
- Prefer the planned crate boundaries from the comprehensive plan: CLI wiring in `ee-cli`, reusable domain/services in `ee-core`, models in `ee-models`, persistence in `ee-db`, output rendering in `ee-output`, and adapters in their own optional crates.
- Keep public contracts stable and versioned. Any data structure that leaves the process through CLI/MCP/hook/export should have a schema and at least one golden fixture.
- If a dependency or upstream Franken project is not ready, implement the smallest honest diagnostic/degraded path rather than faking behavior.
- Document any architectural exception with an ADR-oriented note and a verification hook.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The behavior described by `Spike context pack default format` is implemented or, for spikes, conclusively reported with a clear go/no-go recommendation.
- The implementation preserves local-first, no-Tokio, no-rusqlite, agent-native CLI behavior.
- Public command output, if any, validates against the stable response envelope and does not pollute stdout with diagnostics.
- Relevant unit, integration, golden, contract, or evaluation tests are added and named so future agents can find them.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and the relevant test subset pass after implementation.
- Any degraded or unavailable dependency state returns a stable error/degradation code with a useful next action.
- No silent durable mutation is introduced; write paths have explicit audit, dry-run, idempotency, or curation behavior as appropriate.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- If no executable test is appropriate for this bead, the close reason or a br comment must explain why and identify the nearest bead that provides coverage.
notes:
Original category: Spikes
Original plan ID: EE-284
Original dependency field: EE-147
If this bead reveals that the original plan dependency was ambiguous or incomplete, add a br comment explaining the correction before implementation.
close_reason: Spike complete. GO: use Markdown as the default interactive ee context pack format when the caller did not explicitly request machine output. JSON remains canonical for --json, --robot, hooks, query-file contracts, schema validation, and golden assertions. TOON remains opt-in for token-efficient display, not the default, because docs/toon-output.md says it is a renderer over canonical JSON and not for hooks, MCP, tests, storage, or audit. Evidence reviewed: README TL;DR, Quick Start, and Agent Harness examples all prescribe --format markdown for ee context; ADR 0007 makes context packs the primary UX and requires renderers to be adapter-like; docs/query-schema.md says JSON is canonical while Markdown and TOON are renderers; docs/agent-outcome-scenarios.md usr_pre_task_brief expects both context Markdown and JSON; current code has a Markdown renderer and existing context_pack.md golden coverage. No executable test was appropriate for this no-code spike; nearest executable coverage is tests/golden context_pack.md plus EE-USR-002/Gate 7. Created follow-up bead eidetic_engine_cli-96sa to implement the context-specific default while preserving explicit format precedence.

title: Reality-Check Bridge Plan: Stream 1 — Performance proof via criterion benches
description:
Epic from /reality-check-for-project audit (2026-05-01).

Background — what changed and why:
The comprehensive plan §28 specifies p50/p99 latency targets for 14 operations and explicitly says "These become criterion benchmarks under benches/. CI fails on regression > 30% from the moving baseline." The reality check found: there is no benches/ directory, there is no [[bench]] entry in Cargo.toml, and there is no bead anywhere covering this work. This is a clear NO_BEAD vision gap: the README touts performance numbers but they are unprovable. Fixing this gap proves we mean what we say in the README and lets us catch regressions before users see them.

Vision being delivered:
README's Performance section (e.g. p50 8 ms remember, 38 ms hybrid search, 95 ms context pack at 4k tokens) becomes verifiable on every PR.

Scope (children must cover all of):
1. Add benches/ directory with criterion harness (one bench file per category): remember.rs, search.rs, context.rs, why.rs, link.rs, graph_pagerank.rs, curate_candidates.rs, outcome.rs, import_cass.rs, status.rs.
2. Each bench seeds a deterministic in-memory FrankenSQLite DB + hash embedder, runs the operation 100x with criterion, and captures a stable JSON baseline.
3. Versioned baselines under benches/baselines/v0.<minor>.json keyed by operation. Move baseline forward only when explicit `--bless`-style update is requested.
4. CI step that compiles benches and runs `cargo bench --bench *` in profile mode that aborts on >30% regression vs baseline.
5. New CLI surface: `ee bench --json [--operation <name>]` that runs the in-process harness without criterion (cheap signal for release prep).
6. Unit tests verifying every plan-§28 row has a matching bench file, and that the moving-baseline rule is enforced.

Acceptance:
- benches/ exists; cargo bench --workspace builds and runs locally; CI gate runs in <5 min on a typical PR; baselines committed; ee bench --json passes the walking-skeleton smoke; README "Performance" table is generated from the baseline JSON to keep prose and reality aligned.

Dependencies considered:
None blocking. Can run in parallel with other streams. Should NOT be blocked by gate readiness chores; this IS one of them in spirit but is not currently filed.

Why this matters more than it sounds:
Without benches we cannot honestly call any release production-ready, and we cannot tell whether feature additions are silently regressing the headline UX (context-pack latency in particular). Performance honesty is a North Star principle of "Deterministic by default" combined with "Local-first" — slow local memory destroys the whole pitch.
close_reason: Registered remaining criterion bench targets for link/graph_pagerank/curate_candidates and verified bench-related gates (9457f97).

title: EE-PERF-BENCH-remember: criterion bench for ee remember
description:
Add benches/remember.rs implementing a criterion benchmark for the corresponding plan-§28 operation.

Required setup:
- Use the in-process harness from crate::testing (or extract a shared one if missing)
- Seed an isolated FrankenSQLite database at a temp path with the appropriate fixture (5k memories where applicable, otherwise the smallest fixture that exercises the operation honestly)
- Use the deterministic hash embedder from src/search where semantic is on the path
- Bind LabRuntime so cancellation/timing are stable

Required assertions:
- Group name: ee_remember
- At least three input scales relevant to the op (e.g., empty / 100 / 5000 memories for search; 1k / 4k / 8k token budgets for context)
- Output: criterion JSON to target/criterion/, plus a stable JSON snapshot under benches/baselines/v0.X.json keyed by operation name
- Compare-only mode that fails the bench when p50 regresses by >30% vs the committed baseline

Plan §28 budget for this op (used as the hard ceiling):
- See README "Performance" table and plan §28 for exact p50/p99 numbers; the bench MUST encode the hard ceiling per row in code so a future regression cannot silently raise it.

Tests:
- A unit test asserting that benches/remember.rs compiles and that its criterion group name matches the canonical name 'ee_remember'.
- A regression test that runs the bench in --quick mode and checks p50 stays under the hard ceiling.
close_reason: Implemented criterion benchmark for ee remember with three scales (empty/100/5000), dry-run bench, and baseline entry

title: EE-USR-001: Define north-star agent outcome scenario matrix
description:
Define the canonical agent outcome scenario matrix that future agents use to judge whether EE is actually useful.

Scope:
- Name the core user journeys and their fixture families.
- For each journey, list command sequence, expected response envelope, evidence IDs, degraded branches, redaction expectations, and success signal.
- Include at least these journeys: pre-task brief, in-task retrieval/recovery, post-task learning, degraded/offline trust, privacy/export, and multi-workspace/session continuity.
- Map each scenario to the implementation beads and readiness gates responsible for making it pass.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Treat this as a scenario contract, not prose-only docs. The output should be directly translatable into e2e/golden/eval fixtures.
- Prefer concrete commands and expected JSON field names over vague user stories.
- Include both high-confidence and low-confidence branches so agents learn when to trust EE and when to ask or abstain.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The scenario matrix names every journey, fixture family, command sequence, expected artifacts, degraded branches, and owning beads/gates.
- Each scenario has a clear agent-facing success signal and at least one failure/degraded branch.
- The matrix explicitly states that no scenario may rely on network access, hidden user-global state, or unredacted secrets.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- If no executable test is appropriate for this bead, the close reason or a br comment must explain why and identify the nearest bead that provides coverage.
notes: Added during second bead quality review to prove cross-feature user outcomes, not only subsystem completion.
close_reason: Completed docs-only scenario contract in docs/agent-outcome-scenarios.md and linked it from README.md. No executable test is appropriate for this bead because it defines the scenario matrix; executable coverage is owned by EE-USR-002 through EE-USR-007 and the listed readiness gates.

title: EE-436: Add Gate 20 contract and golden tests for memory economy outputs
description:
Source plan item: EE-436 from the `Memory Economics And Attention Budgets` backlog.

Task: Add Gate 20 contract and golden tests for memory economy outputs

Background and intent:
Treat agent attention as scarce: score utility, cost, false alarms, maintenance debt, and tail-risk reserves before surfacing or demoting artifacts.

Why this matters:
This bead contributes to the overarching goal of making `ee` a dependable local memory substrate for coding agents: useful before work, auditable after work, and safe under degraded conditions. The work should be implemented in the smallest coherent slice that fully satisfies the public contract implied by the title.

Dependency context:
Original plan dependencies: EE-008, EE-431, EE-434. These are also represented as br dependency edges where the referenced bead exists.

Non-negotiable constraints:
Global implementation invariants for this task:
- Keep the CLI local-first, deterministic, and agent-native; stdout is data, stderr is diagnostics.
- Preserve the no-Tokio and no-rusqlite constraint unless a later ADR explicitly changes the architecture.
- Use SQLModel Rust plus FrankenSQLite for durable data; do not introduce a second storage stack.
- Use Asupersync for async edges and cancellation/budget behavior; do not smuggle in a Tokio executor.
- Keep JSON as the canonical public contract. TOON, Markdown, diagrams, and MCP are renderers or adapters over the same domain data.
- Mutating commands need dry-run/idempotency/audit behavior unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- Add focused tests proportional to risk: unit tests for domain logic, contract/golden tests for public output, and integration/evaluation tests for cross-subsystem behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Suggested design approach:
- Tests are part of the deliverable, not a follow-up. Golden output should be deterministic with fixed IDs, timestamps, ordering, and schema versions.

Implementation guidance:
- Read the surrounding modules and existing project conventions before editing.
- Prefer the planned crate boundaries from the comprehensive plan: CLI wiring in `ee-cli`, reusable domain/services in `ee-core`, models in `ee-models`, persistence in `ee-db`, output rendering in `ee-output`, and adapters in their own optional crates.
- Keep public contracts stable and versioned. Any data structure that leaves the process through CLI/MCP/hook/export should have a schema and at least one golden fixture.
- If a dependency or upstream Franken project is not ready, implement the smallest honest diagnostic/degraded path rather than faking behavior.
- Document any architectural exception with an ADR-oriented note and a verification hook.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The behavior described by `Add Gate 20 contract and golden tests for memory economy outputs` is implemented or, for spikes, conclusively reported with a clear go/no-go recommendation.
- The implementation preserves local-first, no-Tokio, no-rusqlite, agent-native CLI behavior.
- Public command output, if any, validates against the stable response envelope and does not pollute stdout with diagnostics.
- Relevant unit, integration, golden, contract, or evaluation tests are added and named so future agents can find them.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and the relevant test subset pass after implementation.
- Any degraded or unavailable dependency state returns a stable error/degradation code with a useful next action.
- No silent durable mutation is introduced; write paths have explicit audit, dry-run, idempotency, or curation behavior as appropriate.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.

title: EE-009: Add `Outcome` to CLI boundary mapping
description:
Source plan item: EE-009 from the `Foundation` backlog.

Task: Add `Outcome` to CLI boundary mapping

Background and intent:
Establish the Rust workspace, core crate boundaries, runtime shell, error model, and test harness that every later subsystem depends on. This is the delivery foundation: no feature work should bypass these invariants.

Why this matters:
This bead contributes to the overarching goal of making `ee` a dependable local memory substrate for coding agents: useful before work, auditable after work, and safe under degraded conditions. The work should be implemented in the smallest coherent slice that fully satisfies the public contract implied by the title.

Dependency context:
Original plan dependencies: EE-004, EE-006. These are also represented as br dependency edges where the referenced bead exists.

Non-negotiable constraints:
Global implementation invariants for this task:
- Keep the CLI local-first, deterministic, and agent-native; stdout is data, stderr is diagnostics.
- Preserve the no-Tokio and no-rusqlite constraint unless a later ADR explicitly changes the architecture.
- Use SQLModel Rust plus FrankenSQLite for durable data; do not introduce a second storage stack.
- Use Asupersync for async edges and cancellation/budget behavior; do not smuggle in a Tokio executor.
- Keep JSON as the canonical public contract. TOON, Markdown, diagrams, and MCP are renderers or adapters over the same domain data.
- Mutating commands need dry-run/idempotency/audit behavior unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- Add focused tests proportional to risk: unit tests for domain logic, contract/golden tests for public output, and integration/evaluation tests for cross-subsystem behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Suggested design approach:
- Runtime boundaries must preserve `Outcome`, request budgets, capability narrowing, and cancellation semantics through Asupersync rather than Tokio.

Implementation guidance:
- Read the surrounding modules and existing project conventions before editing.
- Prefer the planned crate boundaries from the comprehensive plan: CLI wiring in `ee-cli`, reusable domain/services in `ee-core`, models in `ee-models`, persistence in `ee-db`, output rendering in `ee-output`, and adapters in their own optional crates.
- Keep public contracts stable and versioned. Any data structure that leaves the process through CLI/MCP/hook/export should have a schema and at least one golden fixture.
- If a dependency or upstream Franken project is not ready, implement the smallest honest diagnostic/degraded path rather than faking behavior.
- Document any architectural exception with an ADR-oriented note and a verification hook.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The behavior described by `Add `Outcome` to CLI boundary mapping` is implemented or, for spikes, conclusively reported with a clear go/no-go recommendation.
- The implementation preserves local-first, no-Tokio, no-rusqlite, agent-native CLI behavior.
- Public command output, if any, validates against the stable response envelope and does not pollute stdout with diagnostics.
- Relevant unit, integration, golden, contract, or evaluation tests are added and named so future agents can find them.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and the relevant test subset pass after implementation.
- Any degraded or unavailable dependency state returns a stable error/degradation code with a useful next action.
- No silent durable mutation is introduced; write paths have explicit audit, dry-run, idempotency, or curation behavior as appropriate.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.

## Success Criteria

design: Coordinate the `Procedure Distillation And Skill Capsules` implementation tasks. Keep this epic blocked on its child tasks so epic status reflects real implementation progress. Do not implement code directly under this epic without creating or claiming a more specific child bead.
acceptance_criteria:
## Success Criteria

All child beads in `Procedure Distillation And Skill Capsules` are closed, relevant contract/golden/evaluation tests pass, and any category-specific readiness gate is green.

Epic-level user validation requirements:
- Before this epic closes, its child beads that expose user/agent behavior must have concrete unit, golden/schema, e2e, evaluation, replay, or scenario evidence appropriate to their risk.
- The close reason or final comment must summarize implemented user-visible workflows, verification commands, artifact/log locations, degraded states covered, and any intentionally postponed scope with follow-up bead IDs.
- Do not close this epic by code completion alone; close it only when cross-bead integration is demonstrated and no planned feature/functionality was silently dropped.
- If a child bead closes without executable tests, the epic owner must verify that the exception is documented and that nearest-neighbor scenario or contract coverage still protects the user outcome.

title: EE-TST-003: Implement golden/schema contract runner with readable diffs
description:
Build the shared runner that validates public response envelopes, schemas, golden fixtures, and renderer parity.

Background:
EE's public interface is its data contract. Agents will parse `ee status`, `ee search`, `ee context`, `ee why`, `ee doctor`, `ee preflight`, MCP responses, TOON renderings, and readiness outputs. Golden/schema validation must be easy to run and easy to debug.

Scope:
- Validate `ee.response.v1` envelopes and exported schemas.
- Compare canonical JSON golden fixtures with stable ordering and deterministic IDs/timestamps.
- Provide readable diffs that identify JSON pointer path, expected/actual value, schema version, command, and fixture path.
- Support optional renderer parity checks for compact JSON, JSONL, Markdown, TOON, Mermaid, and MCP adapters where relevant.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Start with canonical JSON and schema validation; renderer parity layers should consume the same canonical fixture data.
- Put diagnostics in stderr/artifacts. Stdout from commands under test remains the exact data being validated.
- Preserve enough log context that an agent can identify whether the failure is schema drift, semantic drift, nondeterminism, redaction failure, or command failure.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- A single local command can validate all golden/schema fixtures relevant to the current implementation stage.
- Failure output includes fixture path, command, exit code, JSON pointer/schema path, expected/actual diff, stdout/stderr artifact paths, and likely failure class.
- The runner catches stdout pollution, missing schema fields, unstable ordering, unredacted secrets, unexpected degradation codes, and renderer parity mismatches.
- Gate beads can depend on this runner instead of each building bespoke validation plumbing.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- If no executable test is appropriate for this bead, the close reason or a br comment must explain why and identify the nearest bead that provides coverage.
notes: Added during bead quality review to make validation and failure ergonomics explicit rather than implied.
close_reason: Implemented the shared golden/schema contract runner with readable failure reports and artifact paths; updated agent-facing health/search goldens for current CLI behavior; verified with cargo fmt --check, RCH cargo check --all-targets, RCH cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, RCH cargo test --test agent_golden_baselines, RCH cargo test --test golden, RCH cargo test search::tests, and UBS targeted scan.

description: Scenario-level e2e bead split out of `EE-TST-MEMORY-COMPOSITION-001`. Proves that temporal validity, deterministic autolink candidates, staged remember-time suggestions, explicit memory_links, `ee why`, and graph outputs compose into explainable evidence paths.

title: EE-TST-004: Implement logged e2e script for the walking skeleton
description:
Implement the primary e2e script for the first usable EE workflow.

Background:
The walking skeleton is the earliest proof that EE is useful to an agent: initialize/status/capabilities, remember, search, build context, explain why, and produce a repair plan under degraded conditions. This must run through the real binary, not just unit tests.

Required flow:
- Create an isolated temporary workspace and config home.
- Run `ee init`/workspace setup once that command exists, or the stage-appropriate equivalent.
- Run `ee status --json`, `ee health --json`, `ee capabilities --json`, `ee remember --json`, `ee search --json`, `ee context --json`, `ee why --json`, and `ee doctor --fix-plan --json` as soon as each command exists.
- Validate stdout envelopes, golden fixtures, redaction, degradation codes, and stderr/artifact logging.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Implement as `scripts/e2e_test.sh` or a similarly obvious script, with sub-scenarios so agents can run all tests or one scenario.
- Use strict shell settings, but trap failures to print a concise summary and preserve artifacts.
- Emit human/agent-readable progress to stderr. Keep command stdout captured as artifacts for schema/golden checks.
- Use deterministic fixture data that exercises provenance, scope, redaction, and degraded outputs without network access.
acceptance_criteria:

- The e2e script runs the real `ee` binary against an isolated workspace and leaves no dependence on user-global state.
- Logs include scenario, step, command, cwd, sanitized env overrides, elapsed ms, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and first-failure diagnosis.
- The script validates stdout/stderr isolation and fails on diagnostic leakage into stdout.
- The script can run in CI and locally, can run one named scenario, and documents its own invocation in the test strategy or help output.

title: Epic: Readiness gates and acceptance contracts
description:
Coordination epic for all readiness gates described in the comprehensive plan. Gates are not ordinary feature tasks: they prove the implementation is honest, deterministic, contract-tested, and safe to claim as shipped.

Each gate bead contains the relevant gate text from the plan and depends on the implementation beads most directly responsible for satisfying that gate. A release or milestone should not claim a capability until the corresponding gate bead is closed.

## Success Criteria

Detailed epic closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as the epic's success contract, including cross-bead user validation, evidence, and logging requirements.
design: Keep gate beads blocked on the feature/test beads they verify. Closing a gate requires evidence from br comments or implementation commits that the named contract/golden/evaluation tests pass.
acceptance_criteria:
## Success Criteria

Every gate bead is closed with a reason that names the passing tests or explicit deferral decision.

Epic-level user validation requirements:
- Before this epic closes, its child beads that expose user/agent behavior must have concrete unit, golden/schema, e2e, evaluation, replay, or scenario evidence appropriate to their risk.
- The close reason or final comment must summarize implemented user-visible workflows, verification commands, artifact/log locations, degraded states covered, and any intentionally postponed scope with follow-up bead IDs.
- Do not close this epic by code completion alone; close it only when cross-bead integration is demonstrated and no planned feature/functionality was silently dropped.
- If a child bead closes without executable tests, the epic owner must verify that the exception is documented and that nearest-neighbor scenario or contract coverage still protects the user outcome.
notes: Gate numbering follows the plan: Gate 0, Gate 0A, and Gate 1 through Gate 22.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Mechanical-boundary reconciliation eidetic_engine_cli-o5l2: readiness gates must reject closure for any affected implementation or skill bead that lacks local unit/static checks, real-binary or skill e2e coverage, stdout/stderr isolation checks, and detailed first-failure logging. The only intentional no-test exception is this plan-space reconciliation style of Beads-only task, and even that still needs br/bv verification.

title: Make diagnostics, jobs, daemon, and adapter status report real capabilities only
description:
## Background

Diagnostic and operations commands are easy places for fake confidence to creep in. `status`, `doctor`, `job`, `daemon`, `analyze science-status`, optional MCP/serve adapters, and capability checks should report real probes, real job rows, real schema/index state, and real degraded capabilities. They should not fill the UX with plausible green checks or future-daemon behavior.

These commands are mechanical and belong in the CLI, but their honesty standard must be as strict as the memory commands because agents will use them to decide whether it is safe to continue.

## Acceptance Criteria

See this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field for the detailed completion contract, including unit/static checks, e2e or docs/e2e coverage, detailed logging, and no-feature-loss requirements.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- `ee doctor/status/job/analyze science-status/daemon` and optional adapter health paths report only real probes, persisted job records, configured capabilities, or explicit unavailable states.
- No command emits a healthy/ready/complete status from hard-coded sample data.
- Every failure/degradation includes stable code, severity, evidence/source, and repair command when one exists.
- MCP/serve adapters, if present, are verified as thin schema-preserving wrappers over CLI/core logic and do not duplicate reasoning.
- Unit tests cover missing DB, migration required, stale index, missing CASS, semantic disabled, graph stale, no job rows, unknown job, daemon unavailable, adapter feature disabled, and JSON error shape.
- E2E scripts log workspace, DB/index generations, capability matrix, job IDs, adapter feature flags, degradation codes, repair commands, stdout/stderr paths, schema/golden status, and first-failure diagnosis.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Mechanical-boundary reconciliation eidetic_engine_cli-o5l2: diagnostics, jobs, daemon, and adapter status remain Rust only when they report real probes, persisted job state, feature gates, or explicit degradation. Any qualitative operational interpretation belongs in docs or an agent skill; no mock health or canned capability output should survive.

title: Make memory revise and status health either persist real state or degrade honestly
description:
## Background

Memory revise and status health are core mechanical surfaces. They must never report success or health from placeholders.

Audit anchors: `src/core/memory.rs` has a revise path that returns stub success instead of writing; `src/core/status.rs` documents memory health as a stub until storage is wired.

## Acceptance Criteria

See this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field for the detailed completion contract, including unit/static checks, e2e or docs/e2e coverage, detailed logging, and no-feature-loss requirements.
design: Wire these surfaces to real storage or convert them to dry-run/unavailable until storage methods exist. Keep revision history, tombstone checks, changed-field calculation, health component formulas, and conservative scoring.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- `ee memory revise` either writes a real revision with audit/search-index effects or is dry-run-only with policy-denied non-dry-run behavior.
- `ee status` memory health reads real counts/confidence/provenance/tombstones/staleness or reports unavailable.
- No stub success, stub revision number, or healthy fixture appears in normal output.
- Unit tests cover no changes, tombstoned original, dry-run preview, successful real write if implemented, storage error, empty health, and conservative health score.
- E2E scripts log original/new memory IDs, changed fields, DB generation, index job status, health components, schema/golden status, and first-failure diagnosis.
close_reason: Completed logged memory revise/status honesty closeout. Memory revise remains honest dry-run-only with policy-denied non-dry-run behavior until revision storage exists, and status health is verified against real DB-backed memory counts/components. Added core unit coverage and real-binary logged e2e; RCH fmt/check/clippy and focused tests passed; UBS exited 0.

comments.author: CopperDuck
comments.text: Completed narrow memory revise CLI honesty slice. Changed src/cli/mod.rs to add ee memory revise, route dry-run previews to the existing core revise_memory logic, emit stable ee.response.v1 JSON for dry-run previews with no stub new_id/revision_number, and map non-dry-run revision writes to policy_denied until immutable revision storage exists. Added focused CLI tests for parser coverage, dry-run JSON preview, stdout/stderr isolation, and non-dry-run policy denial. Verification: cargo fmt --check passed; RCH cargo test memory_revise --lib -- --nocapture passed 10/10 after fixing explicit --tag parsing; CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/tmp/ee_copper_6956_check rch exec -- cargo check --all-targets passed; RCH cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings completed remotely with exit 0; git diff --check passed; ubs src/cli/mod.rs exited 0 with heuristic warnings only. Remaining for full bead closeout: decide whether additional real-binary/e2e logging coverage is required for the combined status+memory-revise acceptance.

comments.author: ProudBasin
comments.text: ProudBasin completed the remaining logged e2e/unit closeout for memory revise and status health honesty. Added real-binary test memory_revise_and_status_health_emit_logged_honest_contracts in tests/advanced_subsystem_logged_e2e.rs covering init -> remember -> memory revise --dry-run -> non-dry-run policy denial -> status. The log asserts original memory ID, null new_id/revision_number, changed fields, revision_write_unavailable degradation, policy_denied first-failure diagnosis, DB file existence, memory health counts/components, and search_index high-watermark/status fields. Added core revise unit coverage in src/core/memory.rs for dry-run preview preserving the DB, no-change usage error, tombstoned original denial, and storage-open failure without stub success. Existing status unit coverage already covers empty health and conservative scoring. Verification: focused RCH advanced logged e2e passed; full advanced_subsystem_logged_e2e passed 5/5; RCH core::memory::tests::revise_memory passed 5/5; RCH cargo fmt --check passed; RCH cargo check --all-targets passed; RCH cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings passed; git diff --check passed; UBS on the changed Rust files exited 0 with heuristic warnings only.

title: Move situation classification and plan recipes out of core decision-making
description:
## Background

Task classification and command recipes can help agents, but they are agent-facing reasoning/planning aids. The Rust CLI may expose mechanical metadata, schemas, command catalogs, and explicit heuristic tags, but should not act like an agent goal planner or situation reasoner.

Audit anchors: `src/core/plan.rs` classifies goals and emits command recipes; `src/core/situation.rs` classifies task text, routes to surfaces, and has show/explain stubs.

## Acceptance Criteria

See this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field for the detailed completion contract, including unit/static checks, e2e or docs/e2e coverage, detailed logging, and no-feature-loss requirements.
design: Split the surface. Keep command catalogs, schema exports, fixture evaluation metrics, and explicit low-stakes heuristic tags if clearly labeled. Move task framing, planning recipes, situation explanation, and downstream routing judgment into skills.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- `ee plan` either becomes a mechanical command catalog/recipe registry with explicit static provenance or moves to a skill-facing docs surface.
- `ee situation classify` labels heuristic output as heuristic and avoids acting as a planner; `show/explain` read real records or degrade.
- No command presents keyword classification as confident task understanding.
- Unit tests cover empty text, ambiguous text, deterministic tags, static recipe provenance, and degraded show/explain.
- E2E scripts log command catalog source, classification input hash, heuristic/degraded flags, schema/golden status, and stdout/stderr paths.
close_reason: Completed: plan recipes now expose static command-catalog provenance; situation classification is heuristic-only, non-decisioning, emits no routes, and show/explain degrade without real records. Focused situation/plan tests and gate19 contracts pass; all-targets check passes; all-targets clippy is currently blocked outside this slice in src/cli/mod.rs.

title: Gate 1: Forbidden-Dependency-Clean Feature Tree
description:
Readiness gate from the comprehensive EE plan.

Gate text and intent:
Run dependency audits against the exact planned feature profile:

```bash
cargo tree --edges features
cargo tree --edges normal
```

Pass criteria:

- no `tokio`, `tokio-util`, `hyper`, `axum`, `tower`, `reqwest`, `rusqlite`, `sqlx`, `diesel`, `sea-orm`, `async-std`, `smol`, or `petgraph`

title: Wire recorder tail, follow, links, and import planning to real persisted event data
description:
## Background

The recorder is a foundational mechanical subsystem: append-only event chains, payload hashing, redaction, import planning, tail/follow views, and links to memories/procedures/outcomes. Stubs here block lab, causal, procedure, and preflight from becoming evidence-backed.

Audit anchors: `src/core/recorder.rs` has good hashing/redaction/import planning pieces, but tail/follow return stubs and links use sample data.

## Acceptance Criteria

See this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field for the detailed completion contract, including unit/static checks, e2e or docs/e2e coverage, detailed logging, and no-feature-loss requirements.
design: Implement persisted event retrieval and link listing over the chosen DB/repository surfaces. Preserve dry-run import planning, redaction, payload limits, and hash chains. Do not duplicate CASS storage; import from CASS JSON as planned.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- Recorder start/event/finish/tail/follow/link surfaces have a coherent persistence story or honest unavailable reports.
- Tail/follow never simulate run state from ID prefixes.
- Links list real relationships or returns empty, not samples.
- Event hash chains are stable and verifiable.
- Unit tests cover payload limit, redaction detection, chain continuity, tail empty/nonempty, follow completion, not found, and link filters.
- E2E scripts log event payload hashes, chain status, redaction classes, DB path, stdout/stderr artifacts, and first-failure diagnosis.
notes: CalmDuck 2026-05-03: claimed after bv --robot-triage ranked this as the top P0 ready task. Starting with a narrow recorder-core slice focused on replacing tail/follow/link stubs with persisted or honest empty/unavailable behavior while avoiding currently dirty src/models/recorder.rs unless needed.
close_reason: Recorder tail/follow/link surfaces now return real caller-supplied data or honest empty/unavailable reports; prefix/sample simulations removed and recorder-specific gates passed.

comments.author: CalmDuck
comments.text: CalmDuck closeout: implemented the recorder-core honesty/persistence seam. tail_recording now returns an honest empty no-store snapshot and tail_recording_from_events handles caller-supplied persisted event snapshots with stable filtering, sorting, total_count, has_more, and limit behavior. follow polling no longer simulates run state from run_id prefixes; no-store poll_follow_events returns StoreUnavailable, while poll_follow_events_from_snapshot covers not_found, active waiting, completed, and new-event states. recorder links no longer emit link_sample* rows; list_links returns an honest empty report and list_links_from_records covers deterministic run/type/artifact filtering. add_link now reports a dry-run plan until durable link writes are wired, so it does not claim a store mutation. Existing import dry-run/hash-chain/redaction behavior is preserved. Verification: cargo fmt --check passed; git diff --check passed for recorder/Beads files; UBS src/core/recorder.rs exit 0 with no criticals and heuristic warnings only; RCH recorder unit suite passed 58/58; RCH cargo check --all-targets passed before the concurrent qmu0 tripwire edit landed; RCH recorder_event_spine_contract passed 6/6; RCH advanced_e2e recorder filter passed 14/14; RCH degraded_honesty recorder filter passed 2/2; RCH advanced_subsystem_logged_e2e advanced_subsystems_emit_logged_json_contracts passed 1/1; RCH cargo clippy --lib -- -D warnings completed remotely with exit 0. RCH cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings is currently blocked outside this slice by the active qmu0 reserved surface tests/contracts/preflight_tripwires.rs importing not-yet-present tripwire items; I did not edit that surface.

title: EE-TST-006: Add failure triage, replay artifacts, and redaction-aware log capture
description:
Make test failures and e2e failures easy for future agents to understand and replay.

Background:
A huge test suite without good failure ergonomics slows agents down. EE should treat test failures the same way it treats memory: evidence should be structured, redacted, attributable, and replayable.

Scope:
- Define log artifact bundles for failed e2e scenarios.
- Capture minimal replay metadata: command, args, cwd/workspace, sanitized env, fixture hash, binary version, schema version, database migration version, and relevant feature flags.
- Redact secrets in captured stdout/stderr/artifacts while preserving redaction classes and evidence hashes.
- Provide a one-command replay hint for failed scenarios where practical.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Use a small structured `failure.json` or equivalent summary per failed scenario plus raw captured outputs when safe.
- The failure summary should be optimized for agents: exact next command, exact artifact paths, and likely root-cause category.
- Never hide redaction events. The log should report that redaction happened, class/count/hash, and whether validation used redacted or raw data.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- Failed e2e runs preserve a redacted artifact bundle with enough metadata to replay or diagnose the failure.
- Failure summaries never leak secrets, but include redaction status/classes/counts and stable hashes for evidence correlation.
- The first failure summary is concise enough for an agent status update while deeper artifacts remain available for investigation.
- Replay metadata is deterministic and does not depend on hidden global state.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- If no executable test is appropriate for this bead, the close reason or a br comment must explain why and identify the nearest bead that provides coverage.
notes: Added during bead quality review to make validation and failure ergonomics explicit rather than implied.
close_reason: Added failure_triage.rs with RedactionClass, RedactionLog, redact_content(), ReplayMetadata, FailureCategory, FailureArtifact, TriageRunner. 15 tests covering secret detection, category classification, JSON output, and replay hints. Commit c21332b0.

title: Add dependency and AI-surface audit for the core binary
description:
## Background

The audit found no direct AI/LLM dependencies, but this should become an automated guarantee. The project already bans Tokio/rusqlite/etc.; this bead extends CI/local checks to catch accidental LLM clients, HTTP stacks in core, or prompt-driven execution in the binary.

## Acceptance Criteria

See this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field for the detailed completion contract, including unit/static checks, e2e or docs/e2e coverage, detailed logging, and no-feature-loss requirements.
design: Implement a repeatable audit using Cargo metadata/tree plus source scans. It should be conservative: allow docs and skill files to mention LLMs, but reject runtime dependencies or core command paths that call external AI APIs or require model reasoning beyond approved local search/embed libraries.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- Audit checks direct/transitive dependencies for forbidden crates and AI/API client crates.
- Audit scans core Rust code for OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini/client/chat-completion style calls and fails unless explicitly allowlisted for docs/tests.
- Audit distinguishes project-local skills and documentation from Rust runtime code.
- Audit output includes matched crate/source path, reason, allowlist status, and repair guidance.
- Unit or shell tests cover positive and negative fixtures.
- E2E/CI log records command, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifacts, and first-failure diagnosis.

title: EE-262: Add memory poisoning validation fixtures
description:
Source plan item: EE-262 from the `Trust, Schema Evolution, And Legacy Import` backlog.

Task: Add memory poisoning validation fixtures

Background and intent:

design:
Suggested design approach:
- Tests are part of the deliverable, not a follow-up. Golden output should be deterministic with fixed IDs, timestamps, ordering, and schema versions.
- Privacy and trust rules dominate optimization. Never surface quarantined, secret-bearing, or instruction-like memory as authoritative advice.

Implementation guidance:
- Read the surrounding modules and existing project conventions before editing.
- Prefer the planned crate boundaries from the comprehensive plan: CLI wiring in `ee-cli`, reusable domain/services in `ee-core`, models in `ee-models`, persistence in `ee-db`, output rendering in `ee-output`, and adapters in their own optional crates.
- Keep public contracts stable and versioned. Any data structure that leaves the process through CLI/MCP/hook/export should have a schema and at least one golden fixture.
- If a dependency or upstream Franken project is not ready, implement the smallest honest diagnostic/degraded path rather than faking behavior.
- Document any architectural exception with an ADR-oriented note and a verification hook.

title: EE-CURATE-TTL-001: Add curation candidate TTL, auto-disposition, and backlog health in ee status
description:
Make the curation candidate queue an active system instead of a write-only backlog: TTL, auto-disposition, and a backlog health metric in `ee status`.

Background:
F7 (systems-thinking) and F4 (failure mode) flagged that the curation pipeline as drafted in the bead set has a structural attractor: agents propose candidates faster than humans review them, the queue grows without bound, semantic and procedural memory layers starve of new high-confidence rules, and the user eventually stops trusting the curation surface. EE-180 (curation_candidates table), EE-181 (candidate validation), EE-183/184/185 (`ee curate candidates/validate/apply`), EE-301 (review queue states), and EE-302 (accept/reject/snooze/merge) collectively implement the surface but assume an active human reviewer. Nothing in the bead set defines what happens to a candidate that has been pending for 14, 30, or 90 days; nothing surfaces the backlog health to the agent in `ee status`; nothing triggers the steward to escalate.

Intended scope:
This bead adds three things on top of the existing curation surface, not a parallel one:
- A configurable TTL per candidate state (proposed, validated, snoozed) with deterministic auto-disposition rules: proposed candidates older than TTL with no outcome events auto-snooze; validated candidates older than TTL with high evidence auto-promote (configurable, default off); harmful-flagged candidates older than TTL escalate.
- A `ee status --json` `curation_health` block: counts per state, oldest pending age, mean review latency, escalations open, and the next deterministic action the steward will take.
- A `ee curate disposition --json` command that explains every auto-disposition decision (dry-run by default) and produces an audit entry on apply.

It does not change the basic propose/validate/apply lifecycle. It closes the loop so the curation backlog never silently rots.

Why this matters:
The premise of EE is that procedural memory grows in trustworthiness over time. The curation backlog is the single chokepoint between agent assertion and durable memory. If it is unbounded, the system trends toward "everything is an agent_assertion" and the value of trust classes erodes. F7 modeled this as a positive feedback loop with an obvious dead-loop attractor; this bead breaks the loop with a deterministic, auditable steward action.

Dependency intent:
This bead must wait for the curation candidate schema and validation surfaces, the review queue state/command surface, the trust taxonomy ADR and enum, the config parser/merge layer, `ee status --json`, the steward worker, the operation/audit log path, and the multi-agent concurrent-writer contract. That dependency shape is deliberate: TTL disposition is a high-leverage quality layer, but it should not force earlier curation tasks to guess about trust semantics, policy storage, audit durability, or lock behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the TTL policy, curation_health status block, disposition command, audit behavior, frozen-time tests, property tests, concurrency fixture, and e2e logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Implement the TTL policy as a small typed table (state, action, threshold_seconds, requires_evidence, requires_no_harmful_within) so changes are configuration, not code. Seed default rows in the migration.
- Reuse the existing audit_log shape; do not invent a parallel audit. Add a `event_kind = curation_disposition` discriminator with the policy_id and the deterministic rule fingerprint.
- Run the disposition pass as part of the existing steward worker (Epic: Steward) so it composes with cancellation, budgets, and the optional daemon. In CLI-only mode, run it on `ee curate disposition --apply` and on `ee maintenance` if that umbrella exists.
- Make the curation_health block in `ee status --json` versioned (`schema: ee.status.v<N>`) so consumers can pin. Keep the field present but empty on a fresh workspace; do not omit it.
- Auto-promote stays disabled by default behind config flag `curation.auto_promote = false`. The bead must ship the gate and the disabled default; users opt in. A deterministic experiment harness (EE-USR-004 path) can flip it later.
- Harmful escalation surfaces through the existing `degraded` array; do not invent a new error path. The escalation clears when the human runs `ee curate apply <id>` or `ee curate reject <id>` and the audit cites the original escalation event.
- Composition: depends on EE-180 (table) and EE-181 (validation); depends on EE-201 (steward module) and EE-260 (trust class enum) so dispositions can produce the right trust transitions. Blocks the future Epic: Steward closure gate.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The curation_candidates schema gains TTL-relevant fields (state_entered_at, last_action_at, ttl_policy_id) without breaking existing rows; migration is idempotent and reversible.
- A new ttl_policy table (or config-resident table) stores per-state TTLs and per-state auto-disposition actions. Defaults: proposed=14d->snoozed, validated=30d->prompt-promote, snoozed=90d->retire-with-audit, harmful=7d->escalate. All defaults are overridable via config and visible through `ee config show --json`.
- The steward gets a deterministic curation_disposition job that runs on schedule and on `ee curate disposition --apply`. Every state transition emits an audit log entry citing the candidate id, prior state, new state, ttl_policy_id, ttl_elapsed_ms, evidence_count, and the deterministic rule that fired.
- `ee status --json` includes a stable curation_health block: per-state counts, oldest pending age in days, mean review latency in days, open escalations, and the next deterministic action with its scheduled time.
- `ee curate disposition --json --dry-run` (default) lists every disposition decision with the rule applied, the evidence evaluated, and the planned audit entry. `--apply` runs the deterministic transitions and emits stable JSON for each.
- Auto-promote is gated: it only fires when (a) the candidate has at least N=2 helpful outcomes from distinct sessions, (b) no harmful outcome inside the decay window, (c) the deterministic specificity check passes (links to EE-CURATE-SPEC-001 if added). Otherwise auto-promote becomes auto-prompt (a read-only notice on next `ee status`).
- Harmful candidates older than the harmful TTL escalate by surfacing a stable `degraded` array entry on every `ee status` and a non-zero exit on `ee doctor`. Escalations clear when the human resolves them.
- All TTL math uses deterministic UTC timestamps and is testable with frozen time.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Unit tests cover deterministic TTL evaluation under frozen time, per-state policy lookup, edge cases at boundary (TTL minus 1s, TTL exactly, TTL plus 1s), missing fields, and migration of legacy rows without TTL data.
- Integration tests using a real ee binary seed candidates at known ages, run `ee curate disposition --json --dry-run`, assert the planned transitions, run `--apply`, assert audit log shape, then run a second dry-run to assert idempotency (no further transitions).
- Property tests assert that auto-promote never bypasses the dual-outcome and decay-window gates and that auto-snooze never deletes evidence.
- Adversarial fixtures cover: candidates aged into the future (clock drift), candidates with corrupt or missing audit history, escalations on a candidate whose underlying memory has been retired, and concurrent dispositions by two writers (must compose with EE-CONC-001 advisory lock).
- E2E logs capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, audit log diff, schema validation status, and a first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions prove only machine data leaves stdout for `ee status --json`, `ee curate disposition --json`, and `ee config show --json`.
- Closeout evidence cites integration fixture IDs, the audit log delta, the policy table contents, and the verification commands a future agent would run.
notes: Closes the F7 dead-loop attractor: unbounded curation backlog erodes the value of trust classes. Three-bead system (TTL + status block + disposition command) chosen over a single big change because each piece has a separate test surface and a separate release point. Default policy (14d/30d/90d/7d) chosen to match the README decay half-life of 60d.
