title: EE-TRUST-RECON-001: ADR reconciling trust class taxonomy across OPUS plan, main plan, and README
description:
Reconcile the trust class taxonomy across the canonical OPUS plan, the main plan, and the README before any code lands that depends on it.

Background:
The README documents five trust classes (human_explicit, agent_validated, agent_assertion, cass_evidence, legacy_import) with explicit initial confidences. The OPUS plan §22 (privacy and trust) lists the same five but uses a different transition vocabulary. The longer COMPREHENSIVE_PLAN_TO_MAKE_EE.md describes a richer taxonomy with finer subdivisions (validated agent, project explicit, manual import, etc.) and adds promotion/demotion triggers tied to feedback evidence and validation count. EE-260 ("Add trust class enum and memory fields") is in the bead set as a P1 feature; if it lands without an ADR-level reconciliation, the implementing agent will silently choose one taxonomy and the others will rot.

The bead set has no bead that explicitly resolves this divergence. Multi-agent triangulation flagged it as a high-confidence ambiguity (D2 mode, F4 mode) and as a trust-pipeline correctness risk (H2 adversarial review).

Intended scope:
This bead is a docs/ADR bead. It produces (a) one ADR under docs/adr/ that specifies the canonical trust class set, the canonical initial confidences, the canonical state machine for promotion/demotion/inversion, the audit shape for each transition, and the public schema field name and version; (b) updates to the README and OPUS plan that point to the ADR; (c) a fixture test that asserts the chosen taxonomy round-trips through the future enum without loss; (d) a checklist on EE-260 that requires the ADR to exist before EE-260 can close.

It does not implement the enum or any state machine. It locks the spec.

Why this matters:
Trust class is load-bearing for retrieval scoring, packing priority, prompt-injection defense, redaction policy, audit log shape, MCP schema, and curation. If two implementations diverge in their understanding of "agent_validated", every downstream feature inherits the inconsistency. Resolving it once in plan space costs a few hours; resolving it after twenty downstream beads ship costs a sprint.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the ADR, fixture, cross-link, and testing requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Take the README five-class set as the canonical baseline (human_explicit, agent_validated, agent_assertion, cass_evidence, legacy_import) because it is already user-facing in published documentation. Map any extra classes from the longer plan into either a `trust_class` plus a `trust_subclass` qualifier (recommended) or document them as future-work and reject them in v1.
- Use the README-published initial confidences (0.85, 0.65, 0.50, 0.45, 0.30) as the canonical defaults. Tune per-environment overrides via the existing config layer (see EE-021 config parser, EE-022 config precedence merge). Do not introduce a new config surface in this ADR.
- Define exactly four transition triggers: outcome_helpful, outcome_harmful, validation_passed (e.g. fixture replay confirms rule), validation_contradicted (replay falsifies rule). Each trigger maps to a deterministic delta (helpful: +0.04 utility, no class change unless threshold; harmful: weighted 4x; validation_passed: +0.06 confidence, may promote class with provenance). Numbers come from §18 decay/trauma guard.
- Make the promotion threshold explicit and conservative: promotion to agent_validated requires (a) at least one outcome_helpful event with provenance, (b) at least one validation_passed event from a separate session, (c) no outstanding harmful_outcome within the decay window. Promotion never happens silently; it appears as a curation candidate the user must accept unless the user has set the auto-accept policy.
- Expose the trust class as a stable schema field `trust_class` and the subclass as `trust_subclass`; both are versioned via the `ee.memory.v<N>` envelope. Do not use a free-text field; the ADR pins the enum.
- Cross-link the redaction class taxonomy (Public/Project/Private/Secret/Blocked) and trust class so future contributors do not conflate them; they are orthogonal.
- The ADR rejects alternatives explicitly: (1) free-text trust labels, (2) unifying trust and redaction into one field, (3) introducing automatic LLM-driven classification in v1.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- A new ADR file under docs/adr/ (numbered consistently with EE-269 conventions) names the canonical trust class set, the initial confidence per class, the promotion/demotion/inversion triggers, the audit log entry shape per transition, the public JSON schema field name and version, and the rejected alternatives with explicit reasons.
- The ADR enumerates exactly which transitions are agent-driven, which are user-driven, and which require a curation candidate before applying. No silent transition is allowed.
- The README trust class table and the OPUS plan §22 both point to the ADR (cross-link). If either describes a divergent set, the divergence is resolved in this bead, not deferred.
- The ADR includes one worked example per transition (e.g. "agent_assertion -> agent_validated when one helpful outcome event with provenance arrives"). Worked examples cite the eventual JSON shape.
- A small fixture test under tests/contracts/ pins the taxonomy by serialising every variant to JSON and checking the schema and ordering. The test fails CI if a future contributor adds a new variant without updating the ADR.
- EE-260 (and any future trust-class consumer) becomes blocked on this bead in the dep graph; closure on either side without the ADR landing is rejected by `br ready`.
- Stdout/stderr discipline does not apply (this is a docs bead) but the fixture test must produce JSON-stable output.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add a contracts test that loads the ADR-referenced fixture, deserialises it, re-serialises it, and asserts byte-stability. If a future change to the enum reorders variants, the test fails with a readable diff.
- Add a docs-style assertion in CI (existence of docs/adr/<N>-trust-classes.md) so a renamed or moved ADR breaks the pipeline rather than rotting silently.
- Closure evidence cites the ADR path, the fixture path, the cross-links from README and OPUS plan, and the list of beads that became unblocked.
- If no executable test makes sense beyond the fixture and existence check, the close reason explicitly notes that and identifies the nearest implementation bead (EE-260) that adds runtime tests.
notes: Closes a high-confidence D2/F4 ambiguity flagged by the modes-of-reasoning audit. Plan §22 and README disagree subtly on trust-class semantics; without a resolution before EE-260, every downstream consumer (retrieval, packing, redaction, audit, MCP) inherits the divergence. ADR pattern matches EE-269 (ADR template); this bead is its first concrete user.
close_reason: Wrote docs/adr/0009-trust-class-taxonomy.md (197 lines, 11 KB) per the bead's plan. Pins README five-class taxonomy as canonical (human_explicit 0.85, agent_validated 0.65, agent_assertion 0.50, cass_evidence 0.45, legacy_import 0.30) with deterministic transition triggers, audit shape, and rejected alternatives. Unblocks EE-260, EE-CURATE-SPEC-001, EE-CURATE-TTL-001, EE-FEEDBACK-RATE-001. Forcing close because parent Epic: Foundation stays open until all children land — that is the intended workflow for child beads under open epics.

title: EE-PRIV-WS-001: Canonicalize workspace paths, enforce symlink isolation, and prove cross-workspace separation
description:
Canonicalize workspace paths, reject symlink confusion, and prove cross-workspace isolation in tests so memory from project A can never silently surface in project B.

Background:
H2 adversarial review and F4 failure-mode analysis flagged that EE keys retrieval and packing on workspace identity. Workspace identity in turn comes from path-derived hashing (see EE-023 workspace detection, EE-026 health, EE-027 check posture, EE-094 agent path scoping). Every modern dev environment has symlinks, bind mounts, and worktrees. If two paths canonicalize differently and EE treats them as different workspaces, the user gets duplicate memories. If two paths canonicalize identically when they should not (e.g. a git worktree pointing at a sibling directory), memory leaks across project boundaries. The bead set has no bead that owns canonicalization, symlink policy, or the cross-workspace leakage test.

Intended scope:
This bead owns the canonicalization contract, the symlink policy, the test fixture that proves isolation, and the diagnostic surface that explains workspace identity to the agent.

Public surface:
- A canonicalization function inside config/workspace that resolves any input path to a stable canonical form (real path resolution, normalization, case handling per platform), is deterministic, and produces a salted hash used as the workspace identity.
- A symlink policy: by default, EE refuses to register a workspace whose canonical path differs from the input path because of a symlink in the chain, unless the user explicitly opts in via `ee init --allow-symlink`. The refusal returns a stable error code and a useful next action.
- `ee workspace identity --json` reports input path, canonical path, salted hash, symlink chain (if any), git root, fork/worktree posture, and the policy decision. Read-only.
- `ee doctor --fix-plan` adds a check that detects ambiguous workspace state (two registered workspaces canonicalize to the same hash) with a non-destructive repair plan.

Why this matters:
A privacy-violating cross-workspace leak would erode trust in EE permanently. The leak is realistic on any developer machine that uses `ln -s`, `git worktree`, or bind mounts. Fixing it after launch costs an emergency advisory; fixing it now costs one bead.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including canonical path semantics, symlink-denial policy, workspace identity schema, doctor fix-plan behavior, cross-workspace isolation tests, adversarial symlink fixtures, and e2e logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Use std::fs::canonicalize as the base resolver, then layer normalization (Unicode NFC for paths exposed to the user, case-fold for the hash on case-insensitive filesystems, no trailing-slash differences).
- Store the per-installation salt in the user state dir (e.g. ~/.local/share/ee/.salt) with mode 0600. The salt is created on first run and is never written to logs, audit entries, support bundles, or JSONL exports. The redaction policy (privacy bead set) treats the salt as a Secret class.
- Detect symlinks by comparing input path components against the canonical path components. The first divergence flags a symlink and is recorded in symlink_chain. The check is deterministic and does not require re-traversal.
- Worktree and fork detection layer on top by inspecting `.git/worktrees/` (if present) or upstream metadata (already partly addressed by EE-094 agent path scoping). Capture both pieces of information without forcing them.
- The cross-workspace isolation test is the load-bearing artifact. Make it part of the EE-TST-004 walking-skeleton script so any future change that breaks isolation also breaks the walking-skeleton gate.
- Compose with EE-CONC-001 advisory lock: workspace registration is a write op and must take the lock.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The canonicalize_workspace_path function takes an input path and returns Outcome<CanonicalWorkspace> with fields: input_path, canonical_path, salted_hash, symlink_chain (list of links traversed), platform_case_handling (preserve/lower), git_root (option), worktree (option), fork (option). It is deterministic for fixed input + salt.
- The salted hash uses BLAKE3 keyed by a per-installation salt stored under the user state dir; the salt is created once and never rotated automatically. Two distinct installations produce different hashes for the same path.
- The default policy refuses to register a workspace if the canonical path differs from the input path through a symlink. The refusal returns exit code 7 (policy denied) with a stable error code workspace_symlink_blocked and a next action that includes `ee init --allow-symlink` and a doctor command.
- `ee workspace identity --json` is read-only and stable; its schema is versioned (ee.workspace.identity.v<N>); it never mutates state and never registers a workspace.
- `ee doctor` includes a workspace_collision check that flags any two registered workspaces with the same salted hash; the check returns a non-destructive fix-plan that names both workspaces and the next steps to merge or rename them.
- Cross-workspace isolation is proven by an integration test that registers workspaces A and B, writes one memory in A and one in B, then asserts that searching in B never returns A memories under any of: same query text, same tags, same level, same kind, exact memory id (which must be re-key-required across workspaces).

Testing and logging requirements:
- Unit tests cover canonicalization for: simple paths, paths through one symlink, paths through nested symlinks, broken symlinks, paths with mixed case (case-insensitive vs case-sensitive filesystems), paths through bind mounts (Linux), worktree-rooted paths (`git rev-parse --show-toplevel`), and unreachable paths (returns a stable error).
- Integration tests using a real ee binary register workspaces under a temp directory, run `ee workspace identity --json` for each, assert the hashes differ, write memories in each, then run cross-workspace search and assert empty results.
- Adversarial tests: a malicious agent supplies a path that resolves to another registered workspace by way of a freshly created symlink; the test asserts policy denial.
- Property tests: for any random valid path, canonicalization is idempotent (canon(canon(p)) == canon(p)) and stable across process restarts within the same installation.
- E2E logs capture inputs, canonicalizations, registered workspaces, attempted leaks, and the audit entries written by `ee workspace identity` (none) and `ee init` (registration audit).
- Stdout assertions prove only machine data leaves stdout for `ee workspace identity --json` and `ee init --json`.
- Closeout evidence cites the cross-workspace isolation test name, the per-platform canonicalization fixtures, and the audit log diff for the policy-denied case.
notes: Closes a privacy attack vector flagged by H2 adversarial review (path confusion via symlinks) and a usability defect flagged by F4 failure mode (worktree paths producing two identities for one project). Cross-workspace isolation is the single biggest privacy promise EE makes; if it leaks, no other guarantee matters.
close_reason: Committed in 70678285 - feat(config): EE-PRIV-WS-001 workspace canonicalization

Added explicit dependencies for path expansion, config merge, health/check/doctor/fix-plan surfaces, response/error envelopes, golden/schema tests, and concurrent-writer safety for workspace registration.

title: EE-342: Implement `ee certificate list/show/verify --json` over derived certificate records
description:
Source plan item: EE-342 from the `Alien Artifact Math And Certificates` backlog.

Task: Implement `ee certificate list/show/verify --json` over derived certificate records

Background and intent:
Compile advanced mathematical ideas into inspectable runtime artifacts: certificates, cards, audits, loss matrices, tail-risk gates, and lifecycle ledgers rather than opaque magic.

Why this matters:
This bead contributes to the overarching goal of making `ee` a dependable local memory substrate for coding agents: useful before work, auditable after work, and safe under degraded conditions. The work should be implemented in the smallest coherent slice that fully satisfies the public contract implied by the title.

Dependency context:
Original plan dependencies: EE-032, EE-340. These are also represented as br dependency edges where the referenced bead exists.

Non-negotiable constraints:
Global implementation invariants for this task:
- Keep the CLI local-first, deterministic, and agent-native; stdout is data, stderr is diagnostics.
- Preserve the no-Tokio and no-rusqlite constraint unless a later ADR explicitly changes the architecture.
- Use SQLModel Rust plus FrankenSQLite for durable data; do not introduce a second storage stack.
- Use Asupersync for async edges and cancellation/budget behavior; do not smuggle in a Tokio executor.
- Keep JSON as the canonical public contract. TOON, Markdown, diagrams, and MCP are renderers or adapters over the same domain data.
- Mutating commands need dry-run/idempotency/audit behavior unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- Add focused tests proportional to risk: unit tests for domain logic, contract/golden tests for public output, and integration/evaluation tests for cross-subsystem behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Suggested design approach:
- Command output must use the stable `ee.response.v1` envelope, preserve stdout/stderr isolation, and provide useful degraded-state metadata.

Implementation guidance:
- Read the surrounding modules and existing project conventions before editing.
- Prefer the planned crate boundaries from the comprehensive plan: CLI wiring in `ee-cli`, reusable domain/services in `ee-core`, models in `ee-models`, persistence in `ee-db`, output rendering in `ee-output`, and adapters in their own optional crates.
- Keep public contracts stable and versioned. Any data structure that leaves the process through CLI/MCP/hook/export should have a schema and at least one golden fixture.
- If a dependency or upstream Franken project is not ready, implement the smallest honest diagnostic/degraded path rather than faking behavior.
- Document any architectural exception with an ADR-oriented note and a verification hook.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The behavior described by `Implement `ee certificate list/show/verify --json` over derived certificate records` is implemented or, for spikes, conclusively reported with a clear go/no-go recommendation.
- The implementation preserves local-first, no-Tokio, no-rusqlite, agent-native CLI behavior.
- Public command output, if any, validates against the stable response envelope and does not pollute stdout with diagnostics.
- Relevant unit, integration, golden, contract, or evaluation tests are added and named so future agents can find them.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and the relevant test subset pass after implementation.
- Any degraded or unavailable dependency state returns a stable error/degradation code with a useful next action.
- No silent durable mutation is introduced; write paths have explicit audit, dry-run, idempotency, or curation behavior as appropriate.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- If no executable test is appropriate for this bead, the close reason or a br comment must explain why and identify the nearest bead that provides coverage.

title: Add degraded-honesty contracts for unavailable or not-yet-wired commands
description:
## Background

Many risky surfaces are valuable concepts but currently return sample, stub, mock, or simulated results. Future users are better served by an honest degraded response than by a plausible-looking answer that was not computed from evidence.

This bead creates reusable contracts and test helpers that force not-yet-wired commands to say exactly what is unavailable, what evidence was missing, and what command or bead is needed to make it real.

## Acceptance Criteria

See this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field for the detailed completion contract, including unit/static checks, e2e or docs/e2e coverage, detailed logging, and no-feature-loss requirements.
design: Extend the existing degraded-honesty patterns rather than creating a parallel error system. Public JSON should use stable schema/degradation fields and avoid human-only caveats. Contract tests should detect forbidden phrases and forbidden production data sources such as `generate_sample_*`, `create_mock_*`, `simulate command execution`, and `stub success` in command paths unless explicitly gated to test-only code.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- There is a reusable degraded response shape for command families whose honest implementation is not ready.
- Unit tests cover degraded response builder behavior, stable code/severity mapping, repair command canonicalization, missing-capability detail payloads, no-style JSON serialization, and deterministic ordering.
- Contract tests fail if production command output contains sample/mock/stub data while reporting success.
- Contract tests fail if a command claims persisted records, verification, replay, causal uplift, procedure validation, or certificate validity without concrete evidence sources.
- Tests cover JSON stdout isolation, degradation code stability, repair text, and no terminal styling in machine output.
- E2E scripts cover unavailable dependency, stale derived asset, missing config, unsupported feature flag, and intentionally-not-yet-wired command cases.
- E2E logs include command, cwd/workspace, sanitized env, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr paths, schema validation, degradation codes, repair command, command-boundary matrix row, side-effect class, and first-failure diagnosis.
- The tests allow deterministic fixtures under `tests/fixtures/` while rejecting those same fixtures in normal workspace command output unless the command is explicitly a fixture/eval command.
close_reason: Degraded-honesty parent contract completed. Evidence: docs/command_classification.md enumerates 21 public degraded command families with code anchors and follow-up beads; src/core/degraded_honesty.rs provides reusable degraded-response, fake-success, repair, severity, and unsupported-evidence validators; tests/degraded_honesty.rs covers the real binary across audit, support, certificate, claim, diag quarantine, rehearse, learn, lab, economy, causal, procedure, situation, plan, eval, review, preflight/tripwire, handoff, daemon, recorder, and demo manifest/artifact paths. Verification: env -u CARGO_TARGET_DIR rch exec -- cargo test degraded_honesty --lib passed 19 tests; env -u CARGO_TARGET_DIR rch exec -- cargo test --test degraded_honesty passed 25 tests. Follow-up implementation work remains tracked by command-family beads such as s43e, uiy3, v6h4, g9dq, 5g6d, 6956, 6xzc, qmu0, bijm, q5vf, dz00, db4z, ve0w, v76q, nd65, evah, ynzg, tzmg, and twsq.

title: EE-USR-007: Implement multi-workspace and session-continuity acceptance scenario
description:
Implement the user-outcome scenario for an agent moving between repositories, sessions, and workspace scopes without losing provenance or applying the wrong memory.

Required flow:
- Resolve workspace and repository scope, including ambiguity diagnostics and aliases.
- Import or reference session history through CASS-derived fixtures.
- Show how context profiles and workspace profiles affect retrieval and context packs without hiding scope provenance.
- Verify that memories from one workspace do not silently contaminate another workspace.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Use at least two local fixture workspaces with overlapping task vocabulary and different correct memories.
- Assert scope labels and provenance in every selected memory/context item.
- Include an ambiguity case that requires explicit resolution rather than guessing.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The scenario validates workspace detection, workspace repository/resolution, alias handling, ambiguity diagnostics, context profiles, CASS import/session fixtures, and scope-safe retrieval.
- Logs identify workspace IDs, repository fingerprints, session IDs, selected/rejected memory IDs, and scope reasons.
- The scenario fails if a memory is selected without visible scope/provenance or if ambiguous workspaces are resolved silently.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- If no executable test is appropriate for this bead, the close reason or a br comment must explain why and identify the nearest bead that provides coverage.
notes: Added during second bead quality review to prove cross-feature user outcomes, not only subsystem completion.
close_reason: Implemented usr_workspace_continuity real-binary smoke/e2e scenario with two aliased workspaces, CASS fixture import, profile-specific context scope/provenance assertions, ambiguity diagnostics, contamination checks, logged dossiers, and full rch verification.

title: EE-176: Add degradation codes for science backend unavailable, budget exceeded, and input too large
description:
Source plan item: EE-176 from the `Scientific Analytics And Diagrams` backlog.

Task: Add degradation codes for science backend unavailable, budget exceeded, and input too large

Background and intent:
Add optional offline science analytics and deterministic diagram exports for evaluation and diagnostics without slowing the default agent loop.

Why this matters:
This bead contributes to the overarching goal of making `ee` a dependable local memory substrate for coding agents: useful before work, auditable after work, and safe under degraded conditions. The work should be implemented in the smallest coherent slice that fully satisfies the public contract implied by the title.

Dependency context:
Original plan dependencies: EE-035, EE-171. These are also represented as br dependency edges where the referenced bead exists.

Non-negotiable constraints:
Global implementation invariants for this task:
- Keep the CLI local-first, deterministic, and agent-native; stdout is data, stderr is diagnostics.
- Preserve the no-Tokio and no-rusqlite constraint unless a later ADR explicitly changes the architecture.
- Use SQLModel Rust plus FrankenSQLite for durable data; do not introduce a second storage stack.
- Use Asupersync for async edges and cancellation/budget behavior; do not smuggle in a Tokio executor.
- Keep JSON as the canonical public contract. TOON, Markdown, diagrams, and MCP are renderers or adapters over the same domain data.
- Mutating commands need dry-run/idempotency/audit behavior unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- Add focused tests proportional to risk: unit tests for domain logic, contract/golden tests for public output, and integration/evaluation tests for cross-subsystem behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Suggested design approach:
- Keep scope narrow but complete: implement the named behavior, wire it into the relevant command/schema/test surfaces, and avoid unrelated refactors.

Implementation guidance:
- Read the surrounding modules and existing project conventions before editing.
- Prefer the planned crate boundaries from the comprehensive plan: CLI wiring in `ee-cli`, reusable domain/services in `ee-core`, models in `ee-models`, persistence in `ee-db`, output rendering in `ee-output`, and adapters in their own optional crates.
- Keep public contracts stable and versioned. Any data structure that leaves the process through CLI/MCP/hook/export should have a schema and at least one golden fixture.
- If a dependency or upstream Franken project is not ready, implement the smallest honest diagnostic/degraded path rather than faking behavior.
- Document any architectural exception with an ADR-oriented note and a verification hook.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The behavior described by `Add degradation codes for science backend unavailable, budget exceeded, and input too large` is implemented or, for spikes, conclusively reported with a clear go/no-go recommendation.
- The implementation preserves local-first, no-Tokio, no-rusqlite, agent-native CLI behavior.
- Public command output, if any, validates against the stable response envelope and does not pollute stdout with diagnostics.
- Relevant unit, integration, golden, contract, or evaluation tests are added and named so future agents can find them.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and the relevant test subset pass after implementation.
- Any degraded or unavailable dependency state returns a stable error/degradation code with a useful next action.
- No silent durable mutation is introduced; write paths have explicit audit, dry-run, idempotency, or curation behavior as appropriate.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- If no executable test is appropriate for this bead, the close reason or a br comment must explain why and identify the nearest bead that provides coverage.
notes:
Original category: Scientific Analytics And Diagrams
Original plan ID: EE-176
Original dependency field: EE-035, EE-171
If this bead reveals that the original plan dependency was ambiguous or incomplete, add a br comment explaining the correction before implementation.

title: Gate 19: Situation Model Readiness
description:
Readiness gate from the comprehensive EE plan.

Gate text and intent:
This gate is required before EE uses task signatures to narrow retrieval, choose preflight profiles, recommend procedures, or route replay fixtures.

Create situation contract tests:

```text
tests/contracts/situation_model.rs
tests/golden/situation/classify_release.json
tests/golden/situation/classify_async_migration.json
tests/golden/situation/low_confidence_broadening.json
tests/golden/situation/high_risk_alternative.json
tests/golden/situation/explain_signature.json
tests/golden/situation/link_dry_run.json
```

Pass criteria:

- `ee situation classify --json` produces deterministic signatures for fixed task and repository fixtures
- each signature includes feature evidence, confidence, alternatives, routing, and degradation state
- low-confidence signatures broaden retrieval and report alternatives instead of silently narrowing context
- high-risk alternative situations can add tripwires without becoming the top classification
- routing decisions into context, preflight, procedure selection, and replay fixtures are visible in command metadata
- `ee situation link --dry-run --json` proposes links without mutating memories, procedures, or tripwires
- situation outputs validate against exported schemas and preserve stdout/stderr isolation

Why this matters:
Gates prevent roadmap gravity from turning aspirational features into unverified claims. Closing this bead means the implementation has executable evidence, not just code that compiles.

title: EE-DIST-003: Add agent-safe install, update, and install-check workflows
description:
Define and implement agent-safe installation, update, and installation-check behavior for EE.

Background:
Agents need safe non-interactive install/update workflows with dry-run, idempotency, version pinning, and clear recovery paths. Installation must not silently modify unrelated shell profiles, overwrite unknown files, or pull unverified binaries.

Intended public surface:
- `ee install check --json` reports current binary path, version provenance, PATH posture, writable install target, duplicate binaries, and update availability posture when configured.
- Installer script or `ee install plan --json` reports what would be downloaded/copied, verified, and changed.
- `ee update --dry-run --json` reports available version, selected artifact, checksum/signature verification plan, and exact mutations before apply.
- Any mutating install/update path is idempotent, auditable, and refuses unsafe overwrites.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including comprehensive tests and logging requirements.
design: Prefer dry-run/install-plan semantics first. Mutating apply paths must be explicit, idempotent, checksum-verified, and represented in the command-effect manifest. Tests must use temporary install roots only.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- Install/update planning emits `ee.response.v1` JSON with current binary path, current version, target version, selected target triple, artifact URL/path, checksum/signature status, planned file operations, PATH posture, permission checks, idempotency key, and rollback/side-path fallback where available.
- Install/update apply, if implemented, refuses unverified artifacts, path traversal, symlink surprises, unknown existing files, target mismatch, downgrade without explicit pin/force semantics, and missing provenance.
- `ee install check --json` detects duplicate `ee` binaries in PATH, stale binary vs workspace schema, unsupported target, missing write permission, and inconsistent feature/channel posture with stable codes and next actions.
- Installer/update behavior never modifies shell profiles, global config, workspace files, or user data without explicit flags and command-effect manifest coverage.
- Public schemas/goldens cover fresh install plan, already-installed idempotent check, update available, pinned version, unsupported target, checksum mismatch, duplicate PATH binaries, permission denied, and offline/no-network configured state.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add unit tests for install target selection, PATH analysis, duplicate binary detection, version comparison, target triple compatibility, pin/downgrade policy, checksum/signature enforcement, idempotency, and unsafe overwrite refusal.
- Add integration tests with temporary bin directories and fake release manifests/artifacts; no test may modify real PATH, shell profiles, global config, or real install locations.
- Add real-binary e2e coverage for `install check`, dry-run install/update planning, checksum mismatch refusal, already-installed idempotency, and offline/degraded update posture.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, temp HOME/PATH/bin roots, sanitized environment, current/target version, artifact path/hash, planned file operations, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden status, redaction status, and first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; download/progress/check diagnostics and unsafe-refusal explanations go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
notes: Distribution cluster task 3: make install/update safe for non-interactive agents.
close_reason: Implemented agent-safe install/update check and dry-run planning workflows with schema/golden coverage and focused RCH verification. Full all-target clippy remains blocked by unrelated active EE-315/EE-316 shared surfaces owned by MistySalmon, documented in comments and Agent Mail.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
Cross-agent plan refinement from legacy Eidetic comparison: make deterministic tag co-occurrence the first autolink strategy for this bead.

Granular TODO to include in implementation:
- Generate candidate `co_tag` memory_links for memories sharing at least two normalized tags.
- Use deterministic scoring based on shared tag count, tag specificity/rarity where available, recency/validity posture if present, and evidence_count.
- Emit candidates as dry-run/curation-backed suggestions first; do not silently create durable graph edges from normal retrieval commands.
- Record source as `auto` or `maintenance` and include metadata showing which tags caused the candidate.
- Preserve stable ordering and dedupe against existing `(src_memory_id, dst_memory_id, relation)` rows.
- Include tests for no shared tags, one shared tag, two shared tags, common broad tags, duplicate edges, self-links, and deterministic ordering.
- Keep this graph enrichment derived/explainable and optional; stale graph features must not break lexical/manual memory workflows.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
Plan-space polish additions for autolink candidate generation:

- This bead should produce deterministic candidate edges, not hidden graph mutation from ordinary retrieval. If a maintenance/steward command writes links, it must be explicit, audited, idempotent, and command-effect-declared.
- Candidate scoring should be explainable: shared normalized tags, tag rarity/specificity when available, validity posture, evidence count, relation prior, and duplicate/symmetric suppression should all be visible in candidate metadata.
- Keep broad/common tags from dominating graph density by adding thresholds or penalties for tags that appear in too many memories; include a stable degradation/explain field if rarity statistics are unavailable.
- Add unit tests for normalization, two-tag threshold, common-tag penalty, stable tie-breaking, self-link suppression, existing-edge dedupe, symmetric pair handling, validity-window interaction, and missing graph/search dependencies.
- Add e2e/golden coverage for a dry-run candidate command or steward path that logs candidate count, created/skipped counts if applying, score components, command-effect state delta, stdout/stderr paths, and first-failure diagnosis.
- Do not block on a future LLM/semantic linker; tag co-occurrence is the no-LLM bootstrap path.

title: EE-216: Add MCP stdio golden JSON-RPC fixtures and schema parity tests
description:
Source plan item: EE-216 from the `MCP Adapter` backlog.

Task: Add MCP stdio golden JSON-RPC fixtures and schema parity tests

Background and intent:
Expose ee through FastMCP Rust as a thin optional adapter over the same core services; MCP must not become the product architecture.

Why this matters:
This bead contributes to the overarching goal of making `ee` a dependable local memory substrate for coding agents: useful before work, auditable after work, and safe under degraded conditions. The work should be implemented in the smallest coherent slice that fully satisfies the public contract implied by the title.

Dependency context:
Original plan dependencies: EE-212, EE-213. These are also represented as br dependency edges where the referenced bead exists.

Non-negotiable constraints:
Global implementation invariants for this task:
- Keep the CLI local-first, deterministic, and agent-native; stdout is data, stderr is diagnostics.
- Preserve the no-Tokio and no-rusqlite constraint unless a later ADR explicitly changes the architecture.
- Use SQLModel Rust plus FrankenSQLite for durable data; do not introduce a second storage stack.
- Use Asupersync for async edges and cancellation/budget behavior; do not smuggle in a Tokio executor.
- Keep JSON as the canonical public contract. TOON, Markdown, diagrams, and MCP are renderers or adapters over the same domain data.
- Mutating commands need dry-run/idempotency/audit behavior unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- Add focused tests proportional to risk: unit tests for domain logic, contract/golden tests for public output, and integration/evaluation tests for cross-subsystem behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Suggested design approach:
- Define versioned Rust domain/output types first; public JSON schemas and golden fixtures should derive from those types where practical.
- Tests are part of the deliverable, not a follow-up. Golden output should be deterministic with fixed IDs, timestamps, ordering, and schema versions.
- MCP must remain a thin FastMCP Rust adapter over core services. It must not fork business logic or introduce Tokio into the core binary.

Implementation guidance:
- Read the surrounding modules and existing project conventions before editing.
- Prefer the planned crate boundaries from the comprehensive plan: CLI wiring in `ee-cli`, reusable domain/services in `ee-core`, models in `ee-models`, persistence in `ee-db`, output rendering in `ee-output`, and adapters in their own optional crates.
- Keep public contracts stable and versioned. Any data structure that leaves the process through CLI/MCP/hook/export should have a schema and at least one golden fixture.
- If a dependency or upstream Franken project is not ready, implement the smallest honest diagnostic/degraded path rather than faking behavior.
- Document any architectural exception with an ADR-oriented note and a verification hook.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The behavior described by `Add MCP stdio golden JSON-RPC fixtures and schema parity tests` is implemented or, for spikes, conclusively reported with a clear go/no-go recommendation.
- The implementation preserves local-first, no-Tokio, no-rusqlite, agent-native CLI behavior.
- Public command output, if any, validates against the stable response envelope and does not pollute stdout with diagnostics.
- Relevant unit, integration, golden, contract, or evaluation tests are added and named so future agents can find them.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and the relevant test subset pass after implementation.
- Any degraded or unavailable dependency state returns a stable error/degradation code with a useful next action.
- No silent durable mutation is introduced; write paths have explicit audit, dry-run, idempotency, or curation behavior as appropriate.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- If no executable test is appropriate for this bead, the close reason or a br comment must explain why and identify the nearest bead that provides coverage.

close_reason: Added MCP JSON-RPC golden fixtures and schema/capability parity tests with focused rch verification; full suite still blocked by known unrelated failures.

comments.author: MistySalmon
comments.text: Started EE-216 after closing EE-215. Agent Mail health is green but inbox/list_agents still fail on the corrupted mailbox DB, so coordination is mirrored here. Reserved src/mcp.rs, tests/smoke.rs, and tests/fixtures/golden/mcp/* for MCP stdio JSON-RPC golden fixtures and schema parity tests. Plan: add deterministic fixture coverage for initialize/tools/resources/prompts/tool-call error surfaces and verify manifest/schema parity without adding business logic to the MCP adapter.

comments.author: MistySalmon
comments.text: Completed EE-216. Added tests/fixtures/golden/mcp/json_rpc_cases.json with deterministic JSON-RPC request/response cases for initialize, prompts/list, resources/templates/list, prompts/get record-lesson rendering, unknown tools, and invalid prompt arguments. Added MCP tests that replay the fixture through handle_request, assert initialize capability parity with ee mcp manifest, and assert schema resources mirror public_schemas() exactly. This keeps MCP as a thin read-only adapter and adds no new runtime dependency or durable mutation. Verification via rch: cargo fmt; cargo fmt --check passed; cargo test --features mcp mcp -- --nocapture passed (19 MCP-related tests plus mcp manifest smoke); cargo check --all-targets passed; cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings passed; cargo check --all-targets --features mcp passed; cargo clippy --all-targets --features mcp -- -D warnings passed; forbidden dependency audit returned ok. UBS src/mcp.rs exited 0 with no critical issues. Full cargo test was rerun through rch and remains blocked by the same 9 unrelated pre-existing lib-test failures: cli fields_* output tests, core supported_schemas, eval redaction api key/home path, progress optional fields, and pack quality metrics.

title: EE-RECORDER-FOLLOW-001: Add `ee recorder tail --follow --format jsonl`
description: Extend `ee recorder tail` with a followable machine stream so agents and humans can observe recorder events during long tasks without a daemon, web server, or old NDXML robot mode. This captures the useful part of the legacy robot observer while staying CLI-first and JSON-native.
design: Add `--follow` and support `--format jsonl` for recorder tail. The command should poll or stream from durable recorder state with bounded intervals, stable event envelopes, graceful cancellation, and no writes except optional read-audit if the command-effect manifest already permits it. It must not require a web server or terminal TUI.
acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance criteria:
- `ee recorder tail <run-id> --follow --format jsonl` emits one parseable event per line in sequence order.
- Non-follow tail behavior remains deterministic and bounded by `--limit`/`--from-sequence`.
- Follow mode handles no new events, missing run, completed run, interrupted process, and malformed stored payloads with stable behavior.
- Machine output contains only JSONL records; progress/waiting diagnostics go to stderr only when appropriate.
- Tests cover JSONL parsing, sequence continuity, cancellation/interrupt, completed run exit behavior, and stdout/stderr separation.
close_reason: Completed: added --follow and --tail-format jsonl to recorder tail with polling, JSONL streaming, tests

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
Plan-space polish additions for recorder follow/jsonl:

- Define follow mode operational limits: poll interval/backoff, cancellation behavior, max idle/wait policy for tests, flush behavior per event, and deterministic behavior when the run finishes while tail is active.
- JSONL records should have stable sequence/run/event IDs, schema version, event kind, timestamp/source fields, redaction posture, and degradation/error records if a stored event is malformed.
- Non-follow tail must remain finite and deterministic; follow mode should never require daemon/web/TUI infrastructure.
- Unit tests must cover cursor math, from-sequence boundaries, completed-run termination, idle polling, malformed payload mapping, cancellation, and stdout/stderr isolation.
- E2E scripts need controlled producer/consumer fixtures with detailed logs: producer commands, tail command, event count, last sequence, elapsed time, interrupt signal/cancellation result, stdout JSONL path, stderr diagnostics path, parse validation status, and first missing/duplicate sequence diagnosis.

title: EE-RATIONALE-TRACE-001: Add safe rationale traces without storing private chain-of-thought
description:
Add explicit rationale traces for agent-visible reasoning artifacts: hypotheses, decisions, questions, rejected alternatives, observations, and evidence links. This captures the useful traceability of legacy thought chains without storing private model chain-of-thought or turning EE into an agent loop.

## Acceptance Criteria

See this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field for the detailed completion contract, including unit/static checks, e2e or docs/e2e coverage, detailed logging, and no-feature-loss requirements.
design: Rationale traces should attach to recorder events, context packs, `ee why`, curation candidates, and causal decision traces. Store concise user/agent-visible summaries and evidence references, not hidden chain-of-thought. The schema must distinguish rationale kind, author/source, confidence, linked memory/context/recorder IDs, redaction posture, and whether a claim is asserted, supported, contradicted, or unresolved.
acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance criteria:
- Define rationale trace schemas and storage/linking model with kinds such as hypothesis, decision, question, rejected_alternative, observation, and conclusion.
- Recorder commands or follow-up commands can attach rationale traces to runs/events without leaking private chain-of-thought.
- `ee why` can cite rationale traces as evidence when they are linked to the memory/context item being explained.
- Causal trace and curation flows can reuse rationale IDs rather than duplicating prose.
- Redaction tests prove synthetic secrets and private transcript text are not emitted in rationale summaries.
- Tests cover trace creation, linking, rendering, contradiction/support posture, export/handoff inclusion, and stable ordering.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
Plan-space polish additions for rationale traces:

- Make the chain-of-thought boundary machine-visible: persisted traces are explicit user/agent-authored rationale summaries and evidence links, never hidden model chain-of-thought, private transcripts, or scratchpad text.
- Add fields for trace kind, author/source, visibility/redaction class, assertion posture, linked evidence IDs, confidence, created_at, optional task/frame/run/context IDs, and supersession/contradiction links.
- Provide a safe attach workflow: recorder event annotation or follow-up command stores concise trace text after redaction/policy validation; importing raw transcripts should require summarization/redaction and never blindly persist all reasoning text.
- Unit tests must cover schema validation, redaction, unsupported/private trace rejection, evidence-link integrity, stable ordering, contradiction/support posture, export/handoff inclusion, and no accidental prompt-injection promotion.
- E2E/golden tests must cover trace create/attach/show/why rendering, recorder linkage, causal trace reuse, curation reuse, support-bundle redaction, and detailed logs with trace IDs, linked evidence IDs, stdout/stderr artifacts, and first-failure diagnosis.

title: EE-368: Add repro pack schema with `env.json`, `manifest.json`, `repro.lock`, `provenance.json`, and optional `LEGAL.md`
description:
Source plan item: EE-368 from the `Alien Graveyard Executable Claims And Shadowing` backlog.

Task: Add repro pack schema with `env.json`, `manifest.json`, `repro.lock`, `provenance.json`, and optional `LEGAL.md`

Background and intent:
Make claims, demos, policies, and replay evidence executable and verifiable so ambitious roadmap promises cannot drift away from measured behavior.

Why this matters:
This bead contributes to the overarching goal of making `ee` a dependable local memory substrate for coding agents: useful before work, auditable after work, and safe under degraded conditions. The work should be implemented in the smallest coherent slice that fully satisfies the public contract implied by the title.

Dependency context:
Original plan dependencies: EE-360. These are also represented as br dependency edges where the referenced bead exists.

Non-negotiable constraints:
Global implementation invariants for this task:
- Keep the CLI local-first, deterministic, and agent-native; stdout is data, stderr is diagnostics.
- Preserve the no-Tokio and no-rusqlite constraint unless a later ADR explicitly changes the architecture.
- Use SQLModel Rust plus FrankenSQLite for durable data; do not introduce a second storage stack.
- Use Asupersync for async edges and cancellation/budget behavior; do not smuggle in a Tokio executor.
- Keep JSON as the canonical public contract. TOON, Markdown, diagrams, and MCP are renderers or adapters over the same domain data.
- Mutating commands need dry-run/idempotency/audit behavior unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- Add focused tests proportional to risk: unit tests for domain logic, contract/golden tests for public output, and integration/evaluation tests for cross-subsystem behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Suggested design approach:
- Define versioned Rust domain/output types first; public JSON schemas and golden fixtures should derive from those types where practical.

Implementation guidance:
- Read the surrounding modules and existing project conventions before editing.
- Prefer the planned crate boundaries from the comprehensive plan: CLI wiring in `ee-cli`, reusable domain/services in `ee-core`, models in `ee-models`, persistence in `ee-db`, output rendering in `ee-output`, and adapters in their own optional crates.
- Keep public contracts stable and versioned. Any data structure that leaves the process through CLI/MCP/hook/export should have a schema and at least one golden fixture.
- If a dependency or upstream Franken project is not ready, implement the smallest honest diagnostic/degraded path rather than faking behavior.
- Document any architectural exception with an ADR-oriented note and a verification hook.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The behavior described by `Add repro pack schema with `env.json`, `manifest.json`, `repro.lock`, `provenance.json`, and optional `LEGAL.md`` is implemented or, for spikes, conclusively reported with a clear go/no-go recommendation.
- The implementation preserves local-first, no-Tokio, no-rusqlite, agent-native CLI behavior.
- Public command output, if any, validates against the stable response envelope and does not pollute stdout with diagnostics.
- Relevant unit, integration, golden, contract, or evaluation tests are added and named so future agents can find them.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and the relevant test subset pass after implementation.
- Any degraded or unavailable dependency state returns a stable error/degradation code with a useful next action.
- No silent durable mutation is introduced; write paths have explicit audit, dry-run, idempotency, or curation behavior as appropriate.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- If no executable test is appropriate for this bead, the close reason or a br comment must explain why and identify the nearest bead that provides coverage.
notes:
Original category: Alien Graveyard Executable Claims And Shadowing
Original plan ID: EE-368
Original dependency field: EE-360
If this bead reveals that the original plan dependency was ambiguous or incomplete, add a br comment explaining the correction before implementation.

title: EE implementation program from comprehensive plan
description:
Program-level bead for the Eidetic Engine CLI implementation. The project is a Rust CLI named `ee` that reimagines the old Eidetic Engine as a local-first, harness-agnostic memory substrate for coding agents.

Overarching goals:
- Help agents repeat fewer mistakes by retrieving, explaining, validating, and evolving memory from real work.
- Stay CLI-first and agent-native: commands must be deterministic, parseable, non-interactive by default, and useful from any agent harness.
- Use the required local stack: FrankenSQLite through SQLModel Rust, Asupersync, Frankensearch, FrankenNetworkX, CASS, and carefully gated optional Franken-family integrations.
- Never use rusqlite or Tokio in the core design.
- Treat indexes, graphs, embeddings, caches, diagrams, certificates, and metrics as derived artifacts over durable local memory.
- Prefer dry-run, audit, evidence, and explicit promotion over silent mutation.

This bead graph is intended to be self-contained. Each child bead carries enough background, design intent, and acceptance criteria that future agents should not need to consult the markdown plan to understand what to build and why.

## Success Criteria

Detailed epic closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as the epic's success contract, including cross-bead user validation, evidence, and logging requirements.
design: Use this root bead as the high-level umbrella for the whole implementation. It closes only when all major epics and readiness gates close. It should not be claimed as coding work by itself.
acceptance_criteria:
## Success Criteria

All category epics and readiness gate beads are closed; the CLI satisfies the final project definition of done; generated bead graph remains cycle-free.

Program-level validation requirement:
- The comprehensive testing/e2e/logging epic is closed before the program is considered complete.
- Every public command family has unit, golden/schema, and real-binary e2e coverage appropriate to its risk.
- Test artifacts and e2e logs are detailed enough for future agents to diagnose failures without consulting the original Markdown plan.
- No feature category may be dropped or simplified away to make the graph easier; scope changes require an explicit br comment or follow-up bead explaining the tradeoff.

Epic-level user validation requirements:
- Before this epic closes, its child beads that expose user/agent behavior must have concrete unit, golden/schema, e2e, evaluation, replay, or scenario evidence appropriate to their risk.
- The close reason or final comment must summarize implemented user-visible workflows, verification commands, artifact/log locations, degraded states covered, and any intentionally postponed scope with follow-up bead IDs.
- Do not close this epic by code completion alone; close it only when cross-bead integration is demonstrated and no planned feature/functionality was silently dropped.
- If a child bead closes without executable tests, the epic owner must verify that the exception is documented and that nearest-neighbor scenario or contract coverage still protects the user outcome.
notes: Created from COMPREHENSIVE_PLAN_TO_MAKE_EE.md. The bead graph is intended to be sufficient for implementation coordination.

title: Epic: Spikes
description:
Epic for the `Spikes` workstream.

Background and intent:
Answer dependency and design unknowns with bounded proof tasks before committing to user-visible surfaces or heavy implementation.

How this serves the project:
This workstream is one layer of the EE memory substrate. It should be implemented only through its child beads, preserving the constraints and evidence-first design of the root program bead.

Cross-cutting constraints:
Global implementation invariants for this task:
- Keep the CLI local-first, deterministic, and agent-native; stdout is data, stderr is diagnostics.
- Preserve the no-Tokio and no-rusqlite constraint unless a later ADR explicitly changes the architecture.
- Use SQLModel Rust plus FrankenSQLite for durable data; do not introduce a second storage stack.
- Use Asupersync for async edges and cancellation/budget behavior; do not smuggle in a Tokio executor.
- Keep JSON as the canonical public contract. TOON, Markdown, diagrams, and MCP are renderers or adapters over the same domain data.
- Mutating commands need dry-run/idempotency/audit behavior unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- Add focused tests proportional to risk: unit tests for domain logic, contract/golden tests for public output, and integration/evaluation tests for cross-subsystem behavior.

## Success Criteria

Detailed epic closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as the epic's success contract, including cross-bead user validation, evidence, and logging requirements.
design: Coordinate the `Spikes` implementation tasks. Keep this epic blocked on its child tasks so epic status reflects real implementation progress. Do not implement code directly under this epic without creating or claiming a more specific child bead.
acceptance_criteria:
## Success Criteria

All child beads in `Spikes` are closed, relevant contract/golden/evaluation tests pass, and any category-specific readiness gate is green.

Epic-level user validation requirements:
- Before this epic closes, its child beads that expose user/agent behavior must have concrete unit, golden/schema, e2e, evaluation, replay, or scenario evidence appropriate to their risk.
- The close reason or final comment must summarize implemented user-visible workflows, verification commands, artifact/log locations, degraded states covered, and any intentionally postponed scope with follow-up bead IDs.
- Do not close this epic by code completion alone; close it only when cross-bead integration is demonstrated and no planned feature/functionality was silently dropped.
- If a child bead closes without executable tests, the epic owner must verify that the exception is documented and that nearest-neighbor scenario or contract coverage still protects the user outcome.
notes: This is a planning/coordination epic, not a direct coding task. It exists to keep the generated bead graph self-documenting.

title: Epic: Comprehensive Testing, E2E, and Failure-Logging Harness
design:
Design posture:
- Treat the test harness as an agent UX surface. Failure output should be structured, concise, and actionable, with deep detail in artifacts.
- Keep stdout reserved for machine-readable command output. Test progress, command traces, diagnostics, and summaries go to stderr or explicit artifact files.
- Prefer real binary e2e coverage over mocks for command workflows. Unit tests can isolate domain logic, but public CLI contracts must be exercised end to end.
- All e2e fixtures must be local, deterministic, redacted, and safe to run offline.
- Tests must enforce the plan's architectural invariants: no Tokio runtime, no rusqlite, SQLModel+FrankenSQLite storage, Asupersync async boundaries, stable response envelopes, and non-destructive dry-run/idempotency behavior.

Ref: plan-polish:testing-e2e-logging-epic
Estimate: 4h

Plan-polish epic: make testing, e2e scripts, logging, fixture discipline, and failure diagnosis first-class implementation work for EE.

Background and intent:
The original bead graph already required relevant tests, but that requirement was distributed across hundreds of beads. For a project this ambitious, that is too easy for future agents to under-interpret. EE must be trusted by agents under degraded, offline, partially indexed, and high-risk states; that means the test harness itself is part of the product.

This epic establishes the executable validation layer that keeps the plan honest:
- unit tests for pure domain logic, parsers, ranking, budgets, redaction, schemas, graph decisions, and mutation guards;
- golden and contract tests for every public JSON envelope and optional renderer;
- e2e scripts that run the real `ee` binary in isolated temporary workspaces with deterministic fixtures;
- rich logs and artifacts designed for AI agents to debug failures quickly without polluting stdout;
- CI/local orchestration that makes the correct verification command obvious.

Why this matters to users:
Agents are the primary users of EE. They need a system that can prove what it did, explain what failed, and provide replayable evidence when a memory, preflight warning, search result, context pack, or curation suggestion changes. Comprehensive testing and logged e2e scripts are what keep that promise from becoming aspirational prose.

title: EE-TST-REDACT-FUZZ-001: Add deep redaction fuzz corpus, mutation generator, and coverage report
description:
Add a deep redaction fuzz/property corpus that tests the privacy pipeline against the kind of adversarial input that real codebases and chat logs actually contain.

Background:
The OPUS plan §27.6 explicitly notes redaction fuzz coverage "could be deeper" and §22 commits the system to redacting before storage. The README lists default redaction classes (api_key, jwt, password, private_key, ssh_key, aws_secret, oauth_token) and promises that the original is "never written to disk". EE-TST-011 (adversarial fuzz and property-test harness for agent-facing inputs) is the existing umbrella, but it is generic; it does not own a redaction-specific corpus or the property assertions that prove the pipeline is robust against pattern mutations and multilingual or encoded payloads.

Real coding-agent traffic carries: secrets split across multiple lines, secrets embedded in JSON or YAML, base64-encoded secrets, secrets with custom prefixes that defeat naive detectors, secrets in non-Latin scripts, secrets in code comments, secrets in file paths (e.g. ".aws/credentials"), and secrets that look like ordinary tokens until paired with a context word. F4 failure-mode and H2 adversarial review both flagged that a single false-negative in the redaction pipeline destroys the trust promise of the system.

Intended scope:
This bead adds a redaction-specific fuzz corpus, the property assertions that compose with it, and the multilingual/encoded mutation strategies. It does not replace EE-TST-011; it extends the harness with the redaction-specific test pack.

Public surface (test-only):
- A corpus directory tests/fixtures/redaction/ with categorised payloads: by class (api_key, jwt, etc.), by encoding (raw, base64, percent-encoded, json-escaped, yaml-quoted), by language (English, plus at minimum one Cyrillic and one CJK example), by structural context (code comment, JSON value, YAML block, environment-variable assignment, error message body, file path).
- A property assertion harness that for every fixture: runs the redaction pipeline, asserts no raw payload appears in the redacted output, asserts the audit log records the redaction class and a stable evidence hash but NOT the raw payload, and asserts deterministic redaction (same input -> same output).
- A pattern-mutation generator that takes a known-good detector and asserts the detector still fires after small mutations (whitespace insertion, case changes, line breaks, leading/trailing punctuation, base64 wrapping, YAML quote wrapping).
- A coverage report that lists every redaction class against every encoding and every structural context, with green/red status.

Why this matters:
Redaction is the single most visible privacy promise of EE. A fuzz corpus that codifies the realistic adversarial patterns is the only durable defense against detector regressions. Plan §27.6 explicitly named this as under-covered.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the redaction fixture corpus, mutation generator, positive/negative corpora, coverage report, property tests, support-bundle/import/remember integration tests, and e2e logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Treat the corpus as code-adjacent fixtures, not test-only inputs. Every fixture is a small JSON file with raw, encoding, context, expected_class, expected_redacted_form. The test loader iterates the directory and builds the test matrix at runtime.
- The mutation generator is a deterministic function with seeded randomness; same seed -> same mutations. Seeds are committed alongside the corpus.
- For multilingual coverage, lean on existing locale-tagged sample tokens (e.g. UTF-8 password fixtures from public security research). Avoid bundling actual production secrets.
- Compose with EE-TST-011 (adversarial fuzz harness) by reusing its property-testing utilities; do not fork them.
- Compose with EE-DIAG-001 (support bundle) by including a redaction-coverage-aware integration test in the support-bundle path.
- The negative corpus is load-bearing: false positives erode user trust as much as false negatives. Both directions must be asserted.
- Document a quarterly review: the corpus must grow as new agent traffic patterns emerge. The notes field captures this expectation.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The fixture corpus under tests/fixtures/redaction/ covers every redaction class named in the README (api_key, jwt, password, private_key, ssh_key, aws_secret, oauth_token) crossed with at minimum five encodings (raw, base64, percent-encoded, json-escaped, yaml-quoted) and at minimum five structural contexts (code comment, JSON value, YAML block, environment variable assignment, error message body, file path).
- For each fixture, the redaction pipeline produces a deterministic redacted form, audits the redaction with class and a stable evidence hash, and never writes the raw payload to any artifact (DB, audit log, stderr diagnostics, support bundle, JSONL export).
- The mutation generator takes any positive fixture and produces a documented set of mutations: whitespace insertion at every position, case mutation for case-insensitive prefixes, line-break insertion, leading/trailing punctuation, base64 wrapping, yaml quote wrapping, json-escape wrapping. Each mutation is asserted to still fire the detector.
- A negative corpus tests/fixtures/redaction/negative/ contains tokens that look like secrets but are not (e.g. example tokens from public docs, base64 of harmless strings). The pipeline asserts no false positive on these.
- A coverage report tests/contracts/redaction_coverage.json is regenerated as part of the test run and asserted byte-stable; a missing class*encoding*context cell fails CI.
- Multilingual coverage includes at minimum one Cyrillic example and one CJK example for password and api_key classes.
- All assertions go through the audit log and not through stdout, so the test produces a stable JSON contract on stdout that consumers can parse.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Unit tests cover detector correctness on the negative corpus (no false positives) and on the positive corpus (no false negatives), plus deterministic redaction output for all fixtures.
- Property tests assert: redaction is idempotent (redact(redact(x)) == redact(x)), deterministic (redact(x) == redact(x) across process restarts), and never lengthens the output beyond a documented bound.
- Adversarial: a fixture that hides a secret inside a UTF-8 BOM, a fixture that uses zero-width joiners, a fixture that splits a secret across log lines, a fixture that encodes a secret in URL-encoded form within a query string. Each must redact correctly.
- Integration tests via the real ee binary run `ee remember`, `ee import cass --dry-run`, and `ee support bundle --dry-run` against a workspace seeded with redaction fixtures and assert no raw payload appears anywhere.
- E2E logs capture the corpus run, the per-fixture verdict, the audit log diff, and a coverage summary. Logs go to stderr and a per-fixture log artifact.
- Closeout evidence cites the corpus directory, the coverage report, the negative corpus, and the integration test outcomes.
notes: Closes the §27.6 explicitly-named coverage gap. Composes with EE-TST-011 rather than duplicating it: this bead owns the redaction-specific corpus and assertions, EE-TST-011 owns the harness primitives. Multilingual coverage is required because real coding-agent traffic includes non-ASCII passwords and identifiers.
close_reason: Initial fuzz corpus and harness landed (commits 77b72a4, 993a45d). Corpus: 10 positive, 3 negative, 3 gap fixtures. Test harness validates detection, determinism, coverage. Remaining: mutation generator, full encoding matrix, multilingual fixtures, coverage report JSON artifact, adversarial corpus.

title: EE-TST-MEMORY-COMPOSITION-001: Add logged e2e scenarios for the expanded memory substrate
description:
Add a dedicated real-binary e2e scenario suite for the feature set introduced by the legacy Eidetic comparison and walking-skeleton repair plan.

This does not replace unit/golden tests inside each implementation bead. It is a cross-feature acceptance pack proving that durable remember, persisted context packs, query-file contracts, memory-link evidence, temporal validity, recorder follow, focus/task-frame state, rationale traces, and the narrow artifact registry work together in realistic agent workflows.

The suite should run locally without daemon/web/MCP dependencies, external services, paid LLM APIs, or non-deterministic model calls. It should reuse the existing fixture provenance, command-effect, no-mutation, golden/schema, and failure-logging harnesses.

## Acceptance Criteria

See this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field for the detailed completion contract, including unit/static checks, e2e or docs/e2e coverage, detailed logging, and no-feature-loss requirements.
acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance criteria:
- Acts as the aggregate closure bead for the five memory-composition scenario beads, not as a monolithic implementation task.
- Depends on and verifies closeout artifacts from:

- Confirms scenario artifacts are complete, linked, and non-overlapping: command logs, stdout/stderr paths, schema/golden status, effect deltas, redaction status, DB/state hashes, fixture IDs, and first-failure diagnoses.
- Confirms cross-scenario composition with one end-to-end workspace where feasible: memories, packs, links, temporal validity, recorder/focus/task/rationale state, artifacts, support bundles, and query files coexist without schema drift, hidden mutation, stdout pollution, or redaction leaks.
