title: Epic: Agent Outcome Scenario Acceptance Pack
description:
Plan-polish epic: define and implement end-to-end agent outcome scenarios that prove EE helps real coding agents before, during, and after work.

Background and intent:
The existing beads are granular and feature-rich, which is good for implementation. The remaining risk is that a team could complete many subsystem beads and still not prove that EE feels obvious, useful, conservative, and debuggable to its only intended users: coding agents. This epic adds outcome-level acceptance scenarios that cut across storage, search, context, preflight, recorder, curation, privacy, export, and repair flows.

These are not marketing stories. They are executable acceptance scenarios with commands, fixtures, expected envelopes, degradation behavior, redaction checks, and log artifacts. They should become e2e/golden/evaluation tests as the underlying command families land.

## Success Criteria

Detailed epic closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as the epic's success contract, including cross-bead user validation, evidence, and logging requirements.
design:
Design posture:
- Scenarios should be written from the agent's job-to-be-done: what the agent is trying to decide, what command it runs, what output it consumes, and how it recovers from uncertainty.
- Each scenario should include success, degraded, and failure branches where relevant. EE should be helpful even when indexes are stale, CASS is unavailable, semantic search is disabled, or evidence is incomplete.
- Scenario fixtures must be local, deterministic, redacted, and reusable by e2e scripts, golden contracts, eval harnesses, and documentation examples.
- Keep the scenarios high-fidelity. Do not collapse them into shallow smoke tests; they exist to catch cross-feature integration gaps.
acceptance_criteria:
## Success Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- All child scenario beads are closed and represented by executable fixtures, golden outputs, e2e scripts, evaluation cases, or an explicit staged-skipping rule tied to missing implementation beads.
- The scenario pack covers pre-task briefing, in-task recovery, post-task learning, degraded/offline trust, privacy/export, and multi-workspace/session continuity.
- Each scenario logs command, cwd/workspace, sanitized env, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden result, redaction status, evidence IDs, and first-failure diagnosis.
- Scenario failures produce concrete next actions for agents and link back to the responsible implementation bead or readiness gate.
- Closing this epic demonstrates user-visible value, not only internal subsystem completion.

Epic-level user validation requirements:
- Before this epic closes, its child beads that expose user/agent behavior must have concrete unit, golden/schema, e2e, evaluation, replay, or scenario evidence appropriate to their risk.
- The close reason or final comment must summarize implemented user-visible workflows, verification commands, artifact/log locations, degraded states covered, and any intentionally postponed scope with follow-up bead IDs.
- Do not close this epic by code completion alone; close it only when cross-bead integration is demonstrated and no planned feature/functionality was silently dropped.
- If a child bead closes without executable tests, the epic owner must verify that the exception is documented and that nearest-neighbor scenario or contract coverage still protects the user outcome.
notes: Added during the second bead quality review to make user/agent outcome validation explicit at plan level.
close_reason: Closed after audit of the agent outcome scenario acceptance pack. Direct scenario dependencies are closed, docs/agent-outcome-scenarios.md and docs/fixture-provenance-traceability.md carry the six-scenario executable-evidence matrix, tests/agent_outcome_scenario_pack.rs enforces the closeout/traceability contract, and RCH verification passed: cargo test --test agent_outcome_scenario_pack -- --nocapture (8 passed), cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, and cargo fmt --check.

comments.author: TurquoiseDuck
comments.text: Audit pass: direct dependencies/child scenario beads for w2lk are all closed (gbp2, g2jl, 1mlo, r8r0, 9sd5, jqhn plus supporting testing/fixture beads). Docs inspection confirms docs/agent-outcome-scenarios.md has six scenario rows and executable evidence references, docs/fixture-provenance-traceability.md marks the same six scenario IDs as executable_e2e, and tests/agent_outcome_scenario_pack.rs encodes these matrix/traceability/closure-dossier invariants. git diff --check on the scenario docs and contract test passed. I did not close the epic because the required executable verification is blocked before reaching this crate: RCH cargo test --test agent_outcome_scenario_pack -- --nocapture fails in /data/projects/frankensqlite/crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs:14515 with missing function memdb_row_integer_join_key_with_source. Keep this bead open until that upstream compile blocker is cleared and the contract test can run.

close_reason: Implemented RequestBudget in src/core/budget.rs (576 lines including tests). Type carries wall_clock_deadline, tokens_limit, memory_limit_bytes, io_limit_bytes (all Option-typed; None = unbounded) plus saturating-add monotonic recorded counters. snapshot() and snapshot_at() expose per-dimension (limit, used) as u128 so wall-clock millis and byte counts share a shape. check()/check_at(now) returns Result<(), BudgetExceeded> with deterministic dimension order (WallClock, Tokens, Memory, Io) so two callers seeing the same breach get the same error. BudgetExceeded implements std::error::Error and Display in a format suitable for stderr or future degraded-code mapping by EE-016 / EE-006. Re-exported via src/core/mod.rs. Verification gates (cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test --all-targets, cargo tree -e features) all green: 136 unit tests + 7 forbidden_deps + 6 smoke = 149 passing, no forbidden crates. 18 budget-specific tests cover unbounded, single-dimension breach for each of the four dimensions, simultaneous-breach ordering (wall_clock first; tokens-then-memory-then-io after), saturating arithmetic at u64::MAX, snapshot for unbounded vs bounded dimensions, BudgetDimension::as_str stability, BudgetExceeded Display content, zero-wall-clock immediate breach, and remaining_wall_clock semantics. Strict scope: this bead does NOT thread the budget through command handlers (EE-011) or surface a degraded code (EE-006/EE-016).

title: EE-TST-011: Add adversarial fuzz and property-test harness for agent-facing inputs
description:
Define and implement the project-wide adversarial fuzz and property-test harness for EE's agent-facing inputs, import surfaces, encoders, redaction pipeline, ranking invariants, and repair-plan contracts.

Background:
The backlog already has strong unit tests, e2e scripts, golden/schema contracts, metamorphic evaluation checks, fault/degradation scenarios, command-effect contracts, fixture provenance, and closure dossiers. The remaining testing gap is that malformed or adversarial inputs can still slip between those layers: malformed session history, hostile memory text, weird Unicode/control characters, pathological TOON/JSONL payloads, prompt-injection-like imported content, redaction edge cases, duplicate IDs, oversized fields, unstable ordering, and ranking ties.

Agents are the only intended users of EE, so robustness under strange machine-generated input is not optional. EE will ingest historical agent sessions, user-written memories, generated summaries, logs, JSONL exports, MCP calls, and compact encodings. Those surfaces need adversarial generators and properties, not only hand-written happy-path fixtures.

Scope:
- Define a deterministic fuzz/property-test harness profile that runs locally and in CI without network access or ambient user state.
- Cover parsers and importers for CLI JSON, JSONL exports, TOON renderers/adapters, MCP payloads where applicable, CASS/session-history derived records, legacy Eidetic dry-run scanner outputs, fixture manifests, command-effect manifests, and closure dossiers.
- Cover redaction and privacy properties: synthetic secrets never survive public output; redaction class/count/hash metadata stays stable; redacted output remains schema-valid; adversarial prompt-like content is never promoted to authoritative advice.
- Cover ranking/context/search properties: deterministic ordering under ties, monotonicity where explicitly promised, no quarantined/secret/untrusted memory in authoritative context, stable degradation behavior when optional scorers are disabled, and no ranking changes caused by compact field profiles or output encoders.
- Cover mutation/idempotency properties for command-effect contracts: read-only and dry-run fuzz cases cannot mutate durable state, duplicate idempotency keys cannot double-apply, and failed validation cannot leave partial durable writes.
- Connect generated cases to fixture provenance, failure replay, and closure dossiers so failures are reproducible rather than random blobs.

Why this improves user outcomes:
Agents will feed EE messy, generated, and sometimes hostile text. A dedicated adversarial harness gives future implementers executable guardrails against memory poisoning, secret leakage, nondeterminism, stdout pollution, parser panics, and accidental mutation. This makes EE safer and more predictable in real coding sessions without removing any planned feature.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including comprehensive unit tests, fuzz/property tests, e2e integration, failure replay, and logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Prefer deterministic property tests and bounded fuzz profiles over unbounded randomness. CI should run a fast deterministic profile; a longer local/nightly profile can reuse the same generators and corpus.
- Treat generated failures as fixtures-in-waiting: minimized counterexamples should be saved or referenced through the fixture provenance manifest when they protect an important invariant.
- Suggested generator families: malformed JSON/JSONL/TOON/MCP payloads, hostile memory text, synthetic secrets, weird Unicode/control characters, duplicate IDs, missing schema versions, oversized fields, stale manifest hashes, invalid degradation codes, conflicting evidence, ranking ties, disabled optional scorers, read-only/dry-run command invocations, and legacy import records.
- Suggested properties: no panics, schema-valid errors, stable error/degradation codes, stdout data purity, stderr/artifact diagnostics, deterministic ordering, redaction safety, no authoritative use of quarantined/untrusted content, no hidden mutation for read-only/dry-run commands, idempotency replay correctness, and stable compact-renderer semantics.
- Use shrinking/minimization where the chosen Rust testing crate supports it. If the exact crate is undecided, choose conservatively during implementation and keep the harness compatible with the no-Tokio/no-rusqlite/no-network constraints.
- Logs should be optimized for agents: generator name, seed, shrink path or minimized case path, command/effect expectation, fixture/scenario IDs, manifest hash, stdout/stderr artifact paths, redaction status, state delta, and first invariant violation.
- The harness should integrate with the golden/schema runner and failure replay artifacts rather than inventing separate output conventions.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- A deterministic adversarial fuzz/property-test harness exists for the implemented agent-facing input surfaces and can run in a fast local/CI profile without network access, ambient user config, or nondeterministic wall-clock behavior.
- The harness covers implemented parsers/importers/encoders for CLI JSON, JSONL exports, TOON adapters, MCP payloads where enabled, CASS/session-history records, legacy scanner records, fixture manifests, command-effect manifests, and closure dossiers as those surfaces land.
- Redaction/privacy properties prove synthetic secrets do not appear in stdout, public exports, renderer output, failure summaries, or authoritative context packs; redaction class/count/hash metadata remains stable and schema-valid.
- Ranking/search/context properties prove deterministic ordering under ties, schema-valid degradation when optional scorers are disabled, no authoritative use of quarantined/secret/untrusted memory, and no semantic ranking changes caused solely by compact field profiles or renderers.
- Mutation/idempotency properties prove read-only and dry-run generated cases do not mutate durable state, duplicate idempotency keys do not double-apply, failed validation does not leave partial durable writes, and observed effects match the command-effect manifest.
- Malformed input properties prove no panics, no stdout diagnostics, stable error/degradation codes, bounded resource usage, and actionable next steps for rejected or degraded cases.
- Minimized failures are reproducible through recorded seed, generator name, minimized input or fixture ID, manifest hash, command/effect expectation, and artifact paths.
- CI/local verification exposes a targetable fast profile and a documented extended profile; both report pass/fail/skipped cases with agent-readable summaries.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add unit/property tests for generator correctness, shrink/minimization persistence, duplicate ID rejection, invalid schema version handling, redaction leak detection, ranking tie determinism, renderer semantic stability, and no-mutation snapshot comparison.
- Add e2e or integration coverage that runs the real `ee` binary against at least one adversarial corpus for malformed command input, redaction bypass attempts, import records, and read-only/dry-run effect checks once the relevant command surfaces exist.
- Fuzz/property failure artifacts must capture generator, seed, minimized input path or fixture ID, command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, expected/observed effect, state delta, and first invariant violation.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; fuzz progress, shrink traces, invariant diagnostics, and corpus paths must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- If a generator targets a future surface that is not implemented yet, the harness must record a staged skip tied to the owning bead and fixture manifest entry rather than silently omitting the case.
notes: Added during repeated bead quality review after targeted searches found no dedicated `fuzz`, `adversarial`, or property-test harness bead. This is not a replacement for existing metamorphic evaluation, golden/schema tests, degraded scenarios, effect manifest checks, or fixture provenance. It adds a complementary robustness layer for hostile or malformed machine-generated input, secret leakage, nondeterminism, and accidental mutation.
close_reason: Added fuzz.rs with FuzzRng, 7 adversarial generators, prop_test/assert_no_panic helpers, CI/local/nightly profiles, and 15 unit tests

title: EE-183: Implement `ee curate candidates`
description:
Source plan item: EE-183 from the `Curation` backlog.

Task: Implement `ee curate candidates`

Background and intent:
Generate, validate, review, and apply curation candidates through explicit auditable workflows so automated proposals do not silently rewrite memory.

Why this matters:
This bead contributes to the overarching goal of making `ee` a dependable local memory substrate for coding agents: useful before work, auditable after work, and safe under degraded conditions. The work should be implemented in the smallest coherent slice that fully satisfies the public contract implied by the title.

Dependency context:
Original plan dependencies: EE-180. These are also represented as br dependency edges where the referenced bead exists.

Non-negotiable constraints:
Global implementation invariants for this task:
- Keep the CLI local-first, deterministic, and agent-native; stdout is data, stderr is diagnostics.
- Preserve the no-Tokio and no-rusqlite constraint unless a later ADR explicitly changes the architecture.
- Use SQLModel Rust plus FrankenSQLite for durable data; do not introduce a second storage stack.
- Use Asupersync for async edges and cancellation/budget behavior; do not smuggle in a Tokio executor.
- Keep JSON as the canonical public contract. TOON, Markdown, diagrams, and MCP are renderers or adapters over the same domain data.
- Mutating commands need dry-run/idempotency/audit behavior unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- Add focused tests proportional to risk: unit tests for domain logic, contract/golden tests for public output, and integration/evaluation tests for cross-subsystem behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Suggested design approach:
- Command output must use the stable `ee.response.v1` envelope, preserve stdout/stderr isolation, and provide useful degraded-state metadata.

Implementation guidance:
- Read the surrounding modules and existing project conventions before editing.
- Prefer the planned crate boundaries from the comprehensive plan: CLI wiring in `ee-cli`, reusable domain/services in `ee-core`, models in `ee-models`, persistence in `ee-db`, output rendering in `ee-output`, and adapters in their own optional crates.
- Keep public contracts stable and versioned. Any data structure that leaves the process through CLI/MCP/hook/export should have a schema and at least one golden fixture.
- If a dependency or upstream Franken project is not ready, implement the smallest honest diagnostic/degraded path rather than faking behavior.
- Document any architectural exception with an ADR-oriented note and a verification hook.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- The behavior described by `Implement `ee curate candidates`` is implemented or, for spikes, conclusively reported with a clear go/no-go recommendation.
- The implementation preserves local-first, no-Tokio, no-rusqlite, agent-native CLI behavior.
- Public command output, if any, validates against the stable response envelope and does not pollute stdout with diagnostics.
- Relevant unit, integration, golden, contract, or evaluation tests are added and named so future agents can find them.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and the relevant test subset pass after implementation.
- Any degraded or unavailable dependency state returns a stable error/degradation code with a useful next action.
- No silent durable mutation is introduced; write paths have explicit audit, dry-run, idempotency, or curation behavior as appropriate.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add or update unit tests for core logic introduced or changed by this bead, covering happy path, boundary/empty inputs, invalid inputs, degraded dependencies, deterministic ordering, and idempotency/mutation rules as applicable.
- If the behavior is visible through the CLI, MCP, hook, export, renderer, or evaluation output, add or extend a real-binary e2e, golden, schema, contract, replay, or evaluation fixture that exercises the public surface in an isolated local workspace.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, and a concise first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; diagnostics, progress, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- If no executable test is appropriate for this bead, the close reason or a br comment must explain why and identify the nearest bead that provides coverage.

title: EE-ARTIFACT-REGISTRY-001: Add narrow coding-artifact registry and search links
description: Add a narrow artifact registry for coding-agent work: file/path references, content hashes, MIME/type, redaction posture, extracted text/snippet summaries, provenance, and links to memories/context packs. This intentionally does not import the broad legacy document/OCR/browser pipeline.
design: Keep the scope local and coding-oriented. The registry should track artifacts that agents already produce or cite: files, logs, command outputs, release artifacts, fixture artifacts, and exported bundles. It should hash/dedupe, record safe metadata, optionally store redacted text snippets, and link chunks to memories/search documents. Multi-format PDF/DOCX/OCR and browser ingestion remain out of scope unless a later gate explicitly accepts them.
acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance criteria:
- Define artifact registry schema with artifact ID, workspace/scope, path or external reference, hash, type, size, redaction posture, provenance, and optional extracted safe text/snippets.
- Provide dry-run-first registration/inspection commands or an ADR-backed integration path through existing import/export/support surfaces.
- Search/context can reference registered artifacts through canonical documents without treating derived index data as source of truth.
- Path traversal, symlink escape, oversized file, binary content, secret-bearing content, and missing-file cases return stable errors/degradations.
- Tests cover hash dedupe, redaction, JSONL/export behavior, support bundle interaction, search document generation, and no broad PDF/OCR/browser scope creep.

title: EE-PLAN-001: Add agent goal planner and command recipe resolver
description:
Define and implement a first-class agent goal planner and command recipe resolver for EE.

Background:
The bead graph already includes machine-readable agent-docs recipes, MCP prompt templates, status/health/capabilities, doctor/fix-plan, command-effect manifests, rehearsal sandboxes, audit timelines, and outcome scenarios. Those are strong pieces, but they still require an agent to manually choose which EE workflow applies to the current goal and state.

Agents are the only intended users of EE, so the CLI should make the next safe EE command sequence obvious. A future agent should be able to say: I am starting a task, trying to recover from degraded state, preparing handoff, reviewing a session, importing history, rehearsing a risky change, or closing a bead; EE should return an ordered command plan with preconditions, effect classes, stop conditions, degraded alternatives, and exact follow-up commands.

Intended public surface:
- `ee plan goal --json --goal <text|enum> [--workspace <id>] [--profile compact|full|safe]` maps a natural-language-ish or enum goal to a known recipe and returns a safe ordered command plan.
- `ee plan recipe list --json [--category <cat>]` lists built-in recipe IDs, required capabilities, effect posture, supported profiles, and common failure branches.
- `ee plan recipe show <recipe-id> --json` returns the full recipe, command sequence, required preconditions, expected effects, degraded branches, and e2e scenario references.
- `ee plan explain <plan-id|recipe-id> --json` explains why a recipe was selected, which alternatives were rejected, and what evidence or current posture influenced the plan.

This is not an autonomous executor and not an LLM agent inside EE. It is a deterministic, local, schema-validated recipe resolver over known EE commands, current workspace posture, capabilities, effect metadata, and degraded state. Execution remains explicit; risky plans should point to `ee rehearse run` or dry-run/apply commands rather than silently acting.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including comprehensive unit tests, real-binary e2e scripts, schema/golden fixtures, privacy checks, command-effect checks, and detailed logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- Keep the planner deterministic and bounded. It should select from versioned recipe definitions and current EE posture, not call an external model or invent arbitrary shell commands.
- Define a `goal_plan.v1` schema with plan ID, recipe ID, goal classification, workspace scope, required capabilities, current posture inputs, ordered command steps, expected effect class per step, dry-run/rehearsal recommendation, stop conditions, degraded branches, required confirmations, artifact outputs, and next inspection commands.
- Reuse existing surfaces: agent-docs recipes for examples, command-effect manifest for safety/effects, capabilities/status/health/check/doctor for posture, error-code registry for remediation, rehearsal sandbox for risky sequences, audit timeline for post-run inspection, handoff/support bundles for continuation and debugging, and fixture traceability for scenario evidence.
- Treat goal text as a classifier input with conservative matching. Unknown or ambiguous goals should return candidate recipe IDs and next commands, not a guessed plan.
- Make built-in recipes cover core agent journeys: first run/init, pre-task briefing, in-task retrieval and why, degraded repair, remember/outcome recording, session review/curation proposal, handoff/resume, support bundle capture, backup/export, rehearsal of risky workflows, audit inspection, and closeout evidence.
- Profiles should control verbosity and conservatism, not semantics. `safe` should prefer dry-run/rehearsal and refuse missing effect metadata; `compact` should return fewer fields without changing selected recipe or step ordering.
- Keep stdout as machine data only. Diagnostics, recipe resolution traces, rejected alternatives, and posture collection details go to stderr or artifacts referenced by the response.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance criteria:
- `ee plan goal --json` returns an `ee.response.v1` payload with deterministic goal classification, selected recipe ID, ordered command steps, expected effect classes, dry-run/rehearsal guidance, required capabilities, preconditions, stop conditions, degraded branches, next inspection commands, and rejected alternatives where relevant.
- `ee plan recipe list --json` returns stable recipe IDs, categories, supported profiles, required command surfaces, effect posture, minimum capability requirements, and fixture/scenario references.
- `ee plan recipe show <recipe-id> --json` returns the versioned recipe definition, command sequence spec, required inputs, expected outputs, degraded/failure branches, relevant schemas/goldens, e2e scenario IDs, and links to agent-docs examples.
- `ee plan explain <plan-id|recipe-id> --json` explains selected evidence, current posture inputs, why the selected recipe is appropriate, why rejected alternatives were not chosen, and what the agent should inspect next.
- The planner never executes, mutates memory, imports sessions, changes config, writes backups, runs shell commands, or edits workspace files. It only emits plans and optional explicit rehearsal/dry-run commands.
- Unknown, ambiguous, underspecified, unsupported, or unsafe goals return stable codes, candidate recipe IDs, missing inputs, and next actions instead of guessing.
- Plan outputs are compatible with the command-effect manifest and, where applicable, can emit a command-sequence spec suitable for `ee rehearse plan/run`.
- Built-in recipes cover at least: first run/init, pre-task briefing/context pack, in-task retrieval/why, degraded repair/fix-plan, remember/outcome capture, session review/curation proposal, handoff/resume, support bundle capture, backup/export, rehearsal of risky command sequences, audit timeline inspection, and implementation closeout evidence.
- Public schemas and golden fixtures cover known goal, enum goal, ambiguous goal, unsupported goal, degraded workspace posture, missing capability, safe profile, compact profile, recipe list, recipe show, explain, rehearsal-compatible output, and unknown future recipe version.

Testing and logging requirements:
- Add unit tests for goal classification, recipe matching, ambiguity handling, profile projection, deterministic ordering, capability gating, effect-manifest validation, degraded branch selection, rejected-alternative explanations, recipe version compatibility, and command-sequence-spec rendering.
- Add integration tests against temporary FrankenSQLite/SQLModel workspaces covering fresh workspace, initialized workspace, degraded workspace, missing search index, unavailable optional adapter, ambiguous multi-workspace state, and unknown goal input.
- Add real-binary e2e coverage that runs `ee plan goal --json` for the core built-in recipes, validates schemas/goldens, feeds at least one emitted command-sequence spec into `ee rehearse plan/run`, and proves the planner itself is read-only by comparing source state hashes before and after.
- Add privacy regression tests with synthetic secrets in goal text, workspace names, paths, memory snippets, session-like text, and recipe examples; raw synthetic secrets must not appear in stdout, plans, explanations, logs, or artifacts unless explicitly represented as redacted classes/hashes.
- Add adversarial/property coverage for malformed goals, prompt-injection-like goal text, extremely long input, weird Unicode/control characters, duplicate recipe IDs, invalid recipe versions, missing command-effect entries, stale capabilities, and conflicting degraded-state signals.
- E2E/logged tests must capture command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment overrides, goal input class, selected/rejected recipe IDs, plan ID, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, redaction status, effect-manifest validation status, source-before/source-after hashes, and first-failure diagnosis.
- Stdout assertions must prove stdout contains only machine data; classification traces, posture collection diagnostics, rejected alternative details, warnings, and debugging context must go to stderr or explicit artifacts.
- Closeout evidence must include fixture IDs, scenario IDs, recipe IDs, manifest hashes, command-effect manifest entries, exact verification commands, read-only proof, and the relationship between planner recipes, agent-docs recipes, MCP prompt templates, and rehearsal command specs.
notes: Added during repeated bead quality review after searches found static agent-docs recipes and MCP prompt templates but no first-class deterministic `ee plan` command that turns an agent goal plus current EE posture into a safe ordered command plan. This is intentionally accretive and non-executing: it makes EE easier for agents to use without adding autonomous mutation.
close_reason: Implemented in main: plan goal/recipe/explain commands with 12 recipes. Tests in core::plan::tests pass.

title: Re-scope review, curate, and rule commands to mechanical candidate lifecycle
description:
## Background

`ee review session`, `ee curate`, and `ee rule` are central to the README promise that procedural memory improves over time. The boundary problem is not the lifecycle itself; candidate storage, validation, duplicate checks, evidence counting, feedback aggregation, maturity transitions, tombstones, and audit entries are mechanical. The risky part is automatic distillation or promotion that claims to understand a session without explicit evidence and review.

This bead protects the full curation feature set while splitting generation and judgment cleanly: the CLI owns candidate ledgers and deterministic validation; project-local skills and agent harnesses own synthesis, explanation, and recommendation.

## Acceptance Criteria

See this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field for the detailed completion contract, including unit/static checks, e2e or docs/e2e coverage, detailed logging, and no-feature-loss requirements.
design:
Re-scope curation around explicit artifacts:

- `review session --propose` may import or package CASS evidence spans and create low-confidence candidate shells only when the source data and extraction rule are explicit; otherwise it should hand off to the session-review skill.
- `curate candidates/validate/apply/reject/snooze/merge/retire/tombstone` remain mechanical state transitions with audit logs.
- `rule list/show/add/update/mark` remain mechanical CRUD plus validation, provenance, trust, and decay fields.
- Promotion requires evidence counts, source URIs, validation checks, and explicit apply action; no hidden AI distillation in the binary.

This bead should coordinate with existing trust-model, scoring, memory revision, lab, procedure, and CASS import work rather than creating a parallel curation store.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- Curation and rule commands use persisted records, CASS robot/JSON spans, explicit candidate files, or deterministic validators; normal command output contains no generated wisdom, canned rules, or unsupported session summaries.
- Candidate validation checks specificity, duplication, scope, evidence count, provenance, prompt-injection flags, trust class, and required metadata using documented deterministic rules.
- Apply/reject/snooze/merge/retire/tombstone produce audit entries and never silently mutate procedural memory.
- Any command that would require qualitative distillation returns a skill handoff/degraded report with required evidence inputs.
- Unit tests cover empty candidates, invalid candidate schema, duplicate detection, low-evidence refusal, prompt-injection quarantine, merge audit, tombstone non-deletion, rule state transitions, and idempotent apply/reject behavior.
- E2E scripts log candidate IDs, source URIs, CASS line ranges, validation rule results, trust class, audit entry IDs, mutation summary, stdout/stderr paths, schema/golden status, and first-failure diagnosis.
- Existing curation/rule README functionality remains represented either as mechanical CLI behavior or an explicit skill-assisted workflow.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Mechanical-boundary reconciliation eidetic_engine_cli-o5l2: review/curate/rule stays in Rust only for candidate ledgers, deterministic validation, duplicate/evidence/trust checks, and audited lifecycle mutations. Session distillation and procedure authoring route to skills 0hjw and orz6; no bead in this family should close without curation state-transition tests and logged e2e coverage.

title: Gate 21: Active Learning Agenda Readiness
description:
Readiness gate from the comprehensive EE plan.

Gate text and intent:
This gate is required before EE claims it can actively improve its own memory quality instead of passively accumulating evidence.

Create active learning contract tests:

```text
tests/contracts/active_learning_agenda.rs
tests/golden/learning/agenda.json
tests/golden/learning/uncertainty.json
tests/golden/learning/experiment_propose.json
tests/golden/learning/experiment_run_dry_run.json
tests/golden/learning/observe_negative_result.json
tests/golden/learning/close_inconclusive.json
```

Pass criteria:

- `ee learn agenda --json` ranks questions by expected value and reports uncertainty, target, proposed experiment, and decision impact
- `ee learn experiment propose --json` includes budget, safety boundary, stop condition, dry-run-first flag, and affected decision list
- `ee learn experiment run --dry-run --json` does not mutate memories, procedures, tripwires, situations, economy scores, files, or indexes
- `ee learn observe` records positive and negative evidence without promoting policy changes directly
- inconclusive or unsafe experiments stay auditable and reduce future priority appropriately
- experiments that require human preference or risk tolerance produce `ask_before_acting`
- active learning outputs validate against exported schemas and preserve stdout/stderr isolation

Why this matters:

title: EE-TST-007: Add CI/local verification orchestration and artifact policy
description:
Create the single obvious verification path future agents and CI should run.

Background:
EE's implementation will span many crates and optional adapters. Without a clear verification command, agents will run partial checks and accidentally close beads without proof. This bead defines and implements the local/CI orchestration layer for formatting, linting, unit tests, contract/golden tests, e2e scripts, evaluation fixtures, and artifact retention.

Scope:
- Define canonical commands for local fast checks, full checks, and targeted scenario checks.
- Ensure logs and artifacts are discoverable but not committed accidentally.
- Include detailed stderr progress and final summary lines for agent consumption.
- Document which checks are blocking at each milestone and which are advisory until their subsystem exists.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.
design:
Recommended design:
- A script or cargo xtask may orchestrate checks, but it should still expose individual commands for targeted debugging.
- Keep command names stable. Agents should not need to infer whether to run cargo, shell e2e, schema validation, or eval fixtures separately.
- Make missing optional subsystem tests report `skipped_not_implemented` only before the relevant bead is claimed; after implementation, missing tests should fail.
acceptance_criteria:

title: Gate 15: Counterfactual Memory Lab Readiness
description:
Readiness gate from the comprehensive EE plan.

Gate text and intent:
This gate is required before EE claims it can learn from agent failures rather than merely index them.

Create counterfactual lab contract tests:

```text
tests/contracts/counterfactual_lab.rs
tests/golden/lab/capture_episode.json
tests/golden/lab/replay_baseline.json
tests/golden/lab/counterfactual_add_memory.json
tests/golden/lab/regret_report.json
tests/golden/lab/promote_candidates_dry_run.json
```

Pass criteria:

- `ee lab capture --current --json` stores a redacted episode with pack hash, policy IDs, outcome reference, and repository fingerprint
- `ee lab replay <episode-id> --json` uses frozen episode inputs by default and reports any mutable current-state access
- `ee lab counterfactual` never mutates durable memories, context profiles, policies, or indexes
- all generated curation candidates require normal validate and apply steps
- counterfactual outputs include observed and counterfactual pack hashes, changed items, confidence state, assumptions, degradation codes, and next action
- `wouldHaveSurfaced` means the relevant memory or warning entered the pack, not that the agent certainly would have changed behavior
- redaction policy is enforced before storing episodes, replaying them, or exporting artifacts
- claim output stays `hypothesis` unless backed by replay evidence and verified through `ee claim verify`
- regret reports distinguish missed, stale, noisy, harmful, and overfit-policy regret
- fixed seeds and timestamps produce deterministic golden output
- lab commands preserve stdout/stderr isolation

Why this matters:
Gates prevent roadmap gravity from turning aspirational features into unverified claims. Closing this bead means the implementation has executable evidence, not just code that compiles.

## Acceptance Criteria

Detailed closure criteria are stored in this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field. Treat those criteria as part of this bead's implementation contract, including the comprehensive testing and logging requirements.

title: Mechanical CLI boundary realignment and project-local skill extraction
description:
## Background

The project audit found that `ee` has no direct LLM/API execution dependency, but several command families simulate judgment or emit canned/mock reasoning under serious command names. That violates the intended product boundary even when the implementation is deterministic: `ee` should be a classical, mechanical memory substrate for database reads/writes, indexing, search, packing, replay over frozen evidence, hashing, schema validation, graph metrics, redaction, and audit trails.

Agent harnesses and project-local skills may use intelligence around `ee`; the `ee` binary itself should not pretend to reason, infer causality, distill procedures, plan tasks, or decide what an agent should do unless that result follows from explicit deterministic inputs and persisted evidence.

This epic captures the plan-space work to realign implemented code and open roadmap beads around that boundary without losing any functionality. Features that require judgment move into a project-local `skills/` directory as auditable agent skills that call mechanical `ee` commands. Features that are mechanical stay in Rust but must remove mock/stub/sample behavior, expose honest degraded modes, and include comprehensive unit and e2e coverage with detailed logs.

## Non-negotiable goals

- Preserve all useful product value; do not delete the idea behind features merely because the current implementation is too smart, too fake, or too broad.
- Convert qualitative reasoning surfaces into skills that consume `ee` evidence, rather than baking that reasoning into the CLI.
- Convert mechanical surfaces into real DB/index/replay/hash/schema implementations or explicit unavailable/degraded reports.
- Remove production mock/sample/stub outputs from command paths that agents may trust.
- Add tests and e2e scripts with detailed logging for every migrated command family.
- Keep stdout/stderr and stable JSON contracts intact.
- Keep the no-Tokio/no-rusqlite/no-worktree/no-file-deletion constraints intact.

## Scope

This epic covers command classification, architecture guardrails, Rust CLI re-scoping, project-local skills, and verification. It intentionally happens in plan space first so future implementation agents can work from a complete, self-documenting graph rather than rediscovering the audit context.

## Future-self note

Do not treat this as a simplification pass. The aim is stricter responsibility boundaries, not feature reduction. `ee` should become more trustworthy because every command either computes from concrete evidence or honestly says it cannot.

## Success Criteria

See this bead's structured acceptance_criteria field for the detailed success contract, including unit/static checks, e2e or docs/e2e coverage, detailed logging, and no-feature-loss requirements.
design:
## Design posture

Use a three-way classification for every affected command and bead:

1. **Mechanical CLI**: deterministic Rust implementation over explicit inputs, persisted data, generated indexes, frozen evidence, or local files. These commands may compute scores, aggregate views, validate schemas, replay fixtures, or render packs, but must not invent qualitative conclusions.
2. **Agent skill**: workflows that require synthesis, judgment, task framing, causality interpretation, procedure authoring, or experiment design. These move to project-local `skills/` folders and call `ee` for evidence.
3. **Unavailable/degraded**: command shapes that are planned but not yet implementable honestly. These must return stable JSON degradations, not mock success.

For each command family, preserve the feature intent by splitting it along this boundary. Example: `ee causal trace` can mechanically report exposures/decisions/outcomes from recorder data, while causal interpretation and promotion judgment move into a skill.

The implementation beads under this epic should update existing roadmap beads where necessary, but they must be self-contained enough that future agents can execute without reopening the original audit conversation.
acceptance_criteria:
## Epic acceptance criteria

- A command-by-command boundary inventory exists and classifies every current `ee` command family as mechanical CLI, agent skill, degraded/unavailable, optional adapter, or mixed with explicit split points.
- A command-surface coverage matrix prevents new public commands from bypassing the mechanical-boundary classification.
- A deterministic fixture corpus and golden artifact catalog exists so all tests use explicit evidence rather than ad hoc samples.
- Every public command has a side-effect/idempotency/mutation contract: read-only, append-only, audited mutation, derived-asset rebuild, dry-run/report-only, side-path restore, or degraded/unavailable.
- Runtime budget and cancellation behavior is specified and tested for long-running or multi-stage migrated command paths.
- Baseline infrastructure commands (`init`, `remember`, `outcome`, `import`, `index`, `workspace`, `profile`, `db`, `backup`, `restore`, `export`, `eval`, `completion`, and actual config/status surfaces) are explicitly audited as boring mechanical CLI functionality, not only covered indirectly.
- A README/user-workflow parity matrix proves no advertised user workflow was lost; any deferred behavior has a bead ID and rationale.
- Every affected Rust command family has a child bead that removes production mock/stub/sample outputs or converts them into honest degraded reports.
- Core context/search/pack/why and graph explanations preserve rich retrieval value while proving every score/explanation is deterministic and provenance-backed.
- Review/curate/rule lifecycle work preserves procedural-memory functionality through mechanical candidate ledgers, deterministic validation, explicit apply/reject actions, and audited mutations.
- Diagnostics/jobs/daemon/adapters report real capability probes and job state only.
- Every judgment-heavy feature has a child bead for a project-local skill under `skills/`, including trigger conditions, workflow gates, required `ee` evidence commands, output templates, uncertainty handling, redaction/privacy guardrails, and no unsupported claims.
- The bead graph has no dependency cycles and polish gates block implementation until plan-space review is complete.
- Every child bead includes unit-test requirements and public-surface e2e/golden/logging requirements proportional to risk.
- Cross-cutting contract tests prove that command outputs do not claim evidence that was not read from persisted data, explicit input, or frozen fixtures.
- E2E scripts log command, cwd/workspace, sanitized environment, elapsed time, exit code, stdout/stderr artifact paths, schema/golden validation status, fixture/evidence hashes, side-effect/mutation summary, runtime budget/cancellation details where applicable, redaction status, evidence IDs, degradation codes, command matrix row, and first-failure diagnosis.
- Closing this epic requires a final `br dep cycles` check, `bv --robot-insights` sanity check, and a concise comment summarizing remaining intentional deferrals.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Plan-space bead graph created for the ee mechanical-boundary realignment after the hedge-fund-skills correction. Scope is entirely this project: project-local skills belong under /data/projects/eidetic_engine_cli/skills, and the CLI boundary remains mechanical/algorithmic only. The graph separates inventory/ADR/degraded-honesty foundations, command-family Rust implementation work, project-local skill extraction work, and verification/documentation gates. No beads were created in /dp/hedge_fund_skills_directory; that repository was only inspected for initialization state after the correction.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Polish pass findings and revisions (2026-05-03): I re-reviewed the mechanical-boundary graph against AGENTS.md, README.md, the existing command-family beads, and bv diagnostics. The original split was directionally correct but not complete enough for users. I added six missing beads: eidetic_engine_cli-tzmg for context/search/pack/why and graph explanations as deterministic retrieval; eidetic_engine_cli-ynzg for review/curate/rule lifecycle as mechanical candidate state; eidetic_engine_cli-5g6d for doctor/status/jobs/daemon/adapters reporting real capabilities only; eidetic_engine_cli-frv3 for a command-surface coverage matrix so every ee subcommand is classified; eidetic_engine_cli-g9dq for redacted/provenance-rich evidence handoff into skills; and eidetic_engine_cli-0hjw for session-review/memory-candidate distillation as a project-local skill. I also strengthened the cross-cutting contract/e2e/docs acceptance criteria to cover these surfaces and accepted the relevant bv label suggestions for eidetic_engine_cli-0hjw. The most important graph correction: eidetic_engine_cli-2k2q is now a pre-implementation polish gate instead of being blocked by implementation beads. It depends only on the closed inventory/ADR and blocks the mechanical-boundary implementation, skill, verification, and documentation beads until this review is closed. Current diagnostics: br dep cycles reports zero cycles; bv triage identifies eidetic_engine_cli-2k2q as the mechanical-boundary track blocker; bv plan shows the intended single planning-gate track; bv suggest had no relevant dependency fixes after filtering to this graph, only labels for eidetic_engine_cli-0hjw, which were applied. Ready order after closing the polish gate should be: e2e logging standard (rp3m), degraded-honesty contracts (jp06), project-local skills infrastructure (s7vd), command coverage matrix (frv3), and dependency/AI-surface audit (71ep), followed by redacted skill handoff and the command-family implementation beads. Intentionally deferred: eidetic_engine_cli-o5l2 still needs to reconcile older roadmap beads in detail, but this new graph now gives that work explicit instructions and dependencies. No functionality was dropped; features were preserved as mechanical CLI behavior, project-local skill workflows, or honest degraded states.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Second polish pass (2026-05-03): Reopened eidetic_engine_cli-2k2q and re-reviewed the mechanical-boundary graph against AGENTS.md, README.md, br summaries/full bead text, and bv diagnostics. The main gap was that the previous graph covered smart-sounding command families well but only indirectly protected boring infrastructure commands, shared fixture data, side effects, runtime cancellation, and README workflow continuity. Added five guardrail beads: eidetic_engine_cli-uiy3 for deterministic fixture corpus/golden artifacts; eidetic_engine_cli-s43e for side-effect/idempotency/mutation contracts; eidetic_engine_cli-v6h4 for Asupersync runtime cancellation and budget tests; eidetic_engine_cli-hy6y for baseline infrastructure commands such as init/remember/outcome/import/index/workspace/profile/db/backup/restore/export/eval/completion; and eidetic_engine_cli-myk6 for README workflow parity so no user-facing functionality is lost. Wired these into command-family implementation beads, cross-cutting contracts, e2e suites, docs, roadmap reconciliation, and the epic. Strengthened acceptance criteria on zf1p, frv3, rp3m, twsq, gcru, s38h, 3c93, o5l2, and 2k2q to include fixture hashes, side-effect summaries, runtime budget/cancellation behavior, workflow parity rows, and detailed e2e logging. Accepted bv suggestion: added dependency s38h -> gcru so project-local skill e2e runs after the boundary e2e suite is defined. Rejected bv suggestions: 0hjw -> 00b9 because session review should not depend on counterfactual failure analysis; labels epic/gate/epic-validation-required on 0hjw because it is a skill feature, not an epic or gate. Rejected bv priority decreases for P0 guardrail beads because graph-local direct-unblock scoring understates trust-boundary importance. Diagnostics after changes: br dep cycles reports zero cycles; bv triage still shows 2k2q as the active plan-space blocker; bv insights reports cycles empty and low graph density. Implementation should begin after 2k2q closes with the foundational P0 set: command matrix (frv3), e2e logging (rp3m), degraded honesty (jp06), skills infrastructure (s7vd), dependency/AI audit (71ep), then fixture corpus (uiy3), side-effect contract (s43e), runtime contract (v6h4), baseline command audit (hy6y), and workflow parity (myk6).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
Third polish pass (2026-05-03) focused on local testability and implementation readiness.

What changed:
- Tightened cross-cutting verification beads so each now names concrete unit/static coverage, real-binary or docs/e2e coverage, and detailed first-failure logging. Updated: v6h4, gcru, twsq, rp3m, jp06, g9dq, s7vd, s38h, 3c93, o5l2.
- Tightened every project-local skill bead so each now requires static/unit validation of SKILL.md sections/templates/evidence gates/refusal behavior plus e2e fixtures with evidence bundle hashes, redaction/degraded state, output artifact paths, required-section checks, and first-failure diagnosis. Updated: 0hjw, r5e1, oq6h, orz6, ibuq, 3www, 00b9, 1ki4, 0apr.
- Added test-harness and agent-ergonomics labels where useful for skill/test discoverability.
- Tightened closed planning/ADR/inventory gates so even the closed foundation beads document how unit/static checks, e2e/docs checks, and logs apply: 2k2q, dape, i6vu.

Plan-space validation:
- Read-only br check for mechanical-boundary acceptance criteria now returns no beads missing unit/static, e2e/docs/e2e, or log language.
- br dep cycles --json returned zero cycles.
- bv --robot-triage reports phase2_ready=true, 505 total nodes, 81 open, 41 blocked, 40 actionable, no graph cycles.
- bv --robot-insights computed PageRank, betweenness, HITS, eigenvector, critical path, k-core, articulation, and slack; cycle_break reports cycle_count=0.
- Critical path still starts with frv3 -> s43e -> v6h4 -> command-family migrations -> twsq -> gcru -> s38h -> 3c93 -> zf1p, which is the intended shape.

Relevant bv suggestions reviewed:
- Rejected skill-to-skill dependency suggestions such as 1ki4 -> 0hjw and 0hjw -> 00b9. These skills share labels and evidence vocabulary, but they are independent peer workflows behind the shared s7vd/g9dq/s38h contracts; wiring them together would serialize unrelated skill creation.
- Rejected gate/epic/contract label suggestions for individual skills. They are feature beads for project-local skills, not epics or gates.
- Left broad epic duplicate/dependency suggestions alone because they are unrelated to this mechanical-boundary polish pass and mostly caused by template text similarity across generated plan beads.

Implementation implication:
- The next mechanical-boundary work should continue with the foundations already in progress: frv3 command matrix, rp3m e2e logging, jp06 degraded-honesty, 71ep dependency/AI-surface audit, and s7vd skills standards. Then unblock uiy3 fixtures, s43e side-effect contracts, v6h4 runtime/cancellation, hy6y baseline commands, and myk6 workflow parity.

comments.author: ubuntu

- br lint revealed detailed structured acceptance criteria were not visible to the description-section linter. Added canonical ## Acceptance Criteria / ## Success Criteria pointers in descriptions for open linted beads, without replacing the structured acceptance_criteria source of truth.
- Added rp3m as a dependency for all project-local skill beads and skill contract/infra beads whose acceptance requires logged e2e fixtures: 0hjw, r5e1, oq6h, orz6, ibuq, 3www, 00b9, 1ki4, 0apr, g9dq, s7vd.

- bv --robot-triage reports phase2_ready=true, 505 nodes, 80 open, 38 actionable, 42 blocked, graph has_cycles=false.

bv suggestions reviewed:
- Accepted the substance of rp3m dependency suggestions for skill e2e logging by adding rp3m to all skill/skill-infra beads.
- Rejected skill-to-skill suggestions like twsq->00b9, r5e1->00b9, ibuq->00b9, 3www->00b9; these are template/label similarity noise and would incorrectly serialize independent peer skills.

No feature scope was removed or simplified in this pass; the changes add explicit acceptance visibility, test/logging labels, and dependency constraints only.

title: EE-021: Implement config file parser
description:
Source plan item: EE-021 from the `Config And Workspace` backlog.

Task: Implement config file parser

Background and intent:
Resolve config, paths, workspace identity, status, health, and doctor output in a deterministic way that makes local-first operation reliable.

Why this matters:
This bead contributes to the overarching goal of making `ee` a dependable local memory substrate for coding agents: useful before work, auditable after work, and safe under degraded conditions. Dependency context:
Original plan dependencies: EE-020. These are also represented as br dependency edges where the referenced bead exists.

- Keep the CLI local-first, deterministic, and agent-native; stdout is data, stderr is diagnostics.
- Preserve the no-Tokio and no-rusqlite constraint unless a later ADR explicitly changes the architecture.
- Use SQLModel Rust plus FrankenSQLite for durable data; do not introduce a second storage stack.
- Use Asupersync for async edges and cancellation/budget behavior; do not smuggle in a Tokio executor.
- Keep JSON as the canonical public contract. TOON, Markdown, diagrams, and MCP are renderers or adapters over the same domain data.
- Mutating commands need dry-run/idempotency/audit behavior unless the task explicitly says otherwise.
- Add focused tests proportional to risk: unit tests for domain logic, contract/golden tests for public output, and integration/evaluation tests for cross-subsystem behavior.

design:

comments.author: NobleCardinal
comments.text:
Spike conclusion for EE-097:

Decision: NO-GO for connector-backed normalized conversation import in the default binary right now; GO only for a future explicit opt-in feature/profile after privacy and dependency gates.

Evidence read:
- EE keeps franken-agent-detection as `default-features = false` in Cargo.toml; Gate 8 now freezes that default profile.
- The dependency contract matrix already marks franken_agent_detection connector-backed scans as blocked until privacy and dependency gates.
- `/data/projects/franken_agent_detection` has normalized conversation types behind `connectors`: NormalizedConversation, NormalizedMessage, snippets, invocations, origin/path-mapping. That is enough to map into EE sessions/evidence_spans later.
- Its optional connector profile adds parser infrastructure and optional per-connector features; SQLite-backed connectors use frankensqlite, but enabling connector families changes the dependency and privacy surface.
- Current EE import architecture deliberately consumes CASS as the raw session source (`src/cass/import.rs`) and stores CASS sessions/evidence spans with provenance. Direct connector import would be a second raw-history source and must not silently compete with CASS.

Recommended future shape:
1. Keep `ee agent detect/status/sources/scan-roots` as detection/inventory only in default builds.
2. Add a separate opt-in Cargo feature and config gate for direct connectors, not `all-connectors` by default. Prefer per-agent feature gates so a Cursor/OpenCode SQLite reader does not enable unrelated parsers.
3. Add an `ee import agent-connector --dry-run --json` design before implementation. Dry-run must report connector, source roots, estimated conversations/messages, redaction posture, dependency feature profile, and whether CASS should be preferred.
4. Map NormalizedConversation to EE session/evidence rows only through a new adapter layer that preserves source provenance: connector slug, source path, external id, message idx, content hash, and redaction status.
5. Store raw excerpts only after the same redaction/trust policy used by CASS evidence spans; otherwise persist hashes/metadata and degraded codes.
6. Treat encrypted/private sources such as ChatGPT and SQLite-backed app stores as explicit opt-in per run. No automatic decryption, no background scans, no silent durable mutation.

Follow-up beads recommended before any implementation:
- Contract/golden fixtures for one JSONL connector and one SQLite connector using synthetic private data.
- Forbidden-dependency audit for each connector feature profile.
- Redaction leak tests over NormalizedConversation -> evidence_span mapping.
- A CASS-preferred decision test proving direct import degrades or refuses when CASS can provide the same source with better provenance.

No executable code/test patch is appropriate for this spike because the correct outcome is a gated design decision, not enabling connector import. Whole-repo cargo checks are also currently noisy due active EE-223 reserved-file changes, so this closeout intentionally relies on source/contract inspection and existing Gate 8/default-profile evidence.
